{{Short description|Shinto shrine located in Shijōnawate, Osaka Prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = {{nihongo|'''Shijōnawate Shrine'''|四條畷神社}} | image = Shijonawate Jinja Honden 2018.jpg | caption = Shijōnawate Jinja [[Honden]] | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34.73245|135.64917|type:landmark_region:JP|format=dms|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} '''Shijōnawate Shrine''' (四條畷神社, ''Shijōnawate jinja'') is a [[Shinto]] shrine located in [[Shijōnawate]], [[Osaka Prefecture]], Japan. Its main festival is held annually on February 12. It was founded in 1890, and enshrines [[Kusunoki Masatsura]] along with 24 other [[kami]]. It is one of the [[Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]]. ==Overview== {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Shijonawate Shrine | image = [[image:Shijonawate Jinja Honden 2018.jpg|250px]]
Worship Hall | location = Minamino 2-18-1, [[Shijonawate]], [[Osaka Prefecture]] | coordinates = {{coord|34|43|56.7|N|135|38|57|E|region:JP-27|display=it}} | deity = [[Masatsura Kusunoki]] and 24 others | Shrine rank = [[Bekkaku Kanpeisha]]
[[Beppyo Shrine]] | established = [[1890]] (Meiji 23) | main hall = | alternate names = | Pilgrimage = [[Kenmu Chuko Fifteen Shrines]]
[[Naniwa Seven Fortunes Pilgrimage]]
[[Sacred Sites of the Kinki Region Pilgrimage]] No. 61 (Osaka No. 20) | festival = Spring Grand festival on April 5, Autumn Grand festival on October 5 | ceremony = | map = {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=205|frame-height=200|marker=religious-shinto|type2=shape|fill2=#f5fffa|stroke-color2=#006400|stroke-width2=1|frame-latitude=34.732|frame-longitude=135.649}} {{location map|Japan Osaka Prefecture|float=center|width=205|label=Shijonawate Shrine|position=left|caption=}} }} '''Shijonawate Shrine''' (四條畷神社, Shijōnawate Jinja) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Shijonawate]], [[Osaka Prefecture]]. The shrine was formerly ranked as a [[Bekkaku Kanpeisha]] and is currently listed as a [[Beppyo Shrine]] under the jurisdiction of the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]]. It is one of the [[Kenmu Chuko Fifteen Shrines]] and is selected as one of the [[100 Famous Green Sites of Osaka]]. The shrine enshrines [[Masatsura Kusunoki]], who fought as a commander for the [[Southern Court (Japan)|Southern Court]] and died in the [[Battle of Shijonawate]]. While his father, [[Masashige Kusunoki]], is known as the "Great Kusunoki" (大楠公, Dai Nankō), his eldest son, Masatsura, is known as the "Small Kusunoki" (小楠公, Shō Nankō). Locally, the term "Kusunoki-san" often refers to Masatsura or the shrine itself. In [[1975]] (Showa 50), a town named "Kusunoki" was established, and several other places in the vicinity are named after him. The shrine grounds and surrounding areas are known for their cherry blossoms, attracting many visitors during the spring. == deity == * Main deity - [[Masatsura Kusunoki]] Additionally, 24 other warriors of the Kusunoki clan are enshrined: * [[Masatoki Kusunoki]], [[Masaie Kusunoki]], sons of Masaie Kusunoki * [[Yoshihide Wada]], [[Masatomo Wada]], Wada Kirokuzaemon, sons of Wada Kirokuzaemon * [[Iku Hisahide]], Hatakeyama Yosanosuke Toshiaki, Hatakeyama Rokurō, [[Noda Castle (Kawachi Province)|Noda Shiro]], sons of Noda Shiro * Kane no Shō (某), younger brother of Kane no Shō, Seki Jū Ryōen, son of Seki Jū Ryōen, [[Nishi A (name)|Miwa Nishi A]], son of Miwa Nishi A * Kawabe Ishikimaru, Hōda (某), Amaryōgan, Aoya Gyōbu == History == In [[1348]] (Shōhei 3), the [[Battle of Shijonawate]] took place here, resulting in the defeat of the Southern Court's forces by the Northern Court's army led by the brothers [[Tadayoshi Ashikaga|Tadayoshi]] and [[Takauji Ashikaga|Takauji]]. Masatsura Kusunoki and his brother [[Masatoki Kusunoki|Masatoki]] committed suicide, along with many others from the Kusunoki clan. Afterward, a tomb called "Kusunotsuka" was built for Masatsura in Nanino Village, and his spirit was quietly enshrined. In the [[Meiji era]], the government recognized the Southern Court as the legitimate ruling entity, leading to the deification of Masatsura's father, [[Masashige Kusunoki]], as the "Great Kusunoki" (大楠公). Masatsura, who followed his father's will and fought for the Southern Court, was also revered as the "Small Kusunoki" (小楠公). In [[1878]] (Meiji 11), his tomb was renamed "Small Kusunoki Imperial Mausoleum" and expanded. Around the same time, officials from Sumiyoshi Hirata Shrine near Mount Iimori petitioned the government to establish a shrine for the Kusunoki clan. The petition was granted, and on December 16, [[1889]] (Meiji 22), the founding of Shijonawate Shrine and its designation as a [[Bekkaku Kanpeisha]] were decreed[{{NDLDC|2945191}} Meiji 22 December 17 Official Gazette (No. 1942)] Ministry of the Interior Notice No. 61. The shrine was established in [[1890]] (Meiji 23) next to Sumiyoshi Hirata Shrine. The area around the shrine flourished, particularly after the Naniwa Railway opened a line from central [[Osaka]] to nearby Shijonawate Station in [[1895]] (Meiji 28). The name "Shijonawate" became associated with the area, and in [[1932]] (Showa 7), the village of Koka was renamed Shijonawate Village, later becoming Shijonawate City. The surrounding region developed into one of the largest urban areas in northern [[Kawachi Province]], a status it maintains today. In [[1948]] (Showa 23), the shrine was included in the list of [[Beppyo Shrines]] by the Association of Shinto Shrines. To the west of the shrine lies the Small Kusunoki Imperial Mausoleum (an Osaka Prefecture designated historic site), and to the northwest is the tomb of [[Yoshihide Wada]] (also a designated historic site). == Shrine Grounds == * main hall * Worship Hall * Statue of Masashige and Masatsura Kusunoki, depicting their farewell at Sakurai - Inscribed with "Loyalty and Filial Piety." * Miboyashi Shrine - Enshrines Kusunoki's mother, Kusako Taji no Mikoto. Sub-shrine. * Worship Place * Statue of Masatsura and his mother Kusako - Inscribed with "Loyalty and Filial Piety." * Torii - Gifted from Ise Grand Shrine in [[1990]] (Heisei 2) to commemorate the shrine's 100th anniversary. * Kusunoki Tenjin Shrine - Enshrines [[Sugawara no Michizane]] * Yugen Reikonsha - Enshrines the spirits of ancient and modern scholars and leaders. Originally enshrined at the Adult Education Training Center on the eastern side of Mount Iimori, it was relocated to the current site on April 7, [[2007]] (Heisei 19). * Kusunoki Memorial Tower - A memorial tower for Masashige Kusunoki. It has an unusual design with two round stones representing the water element, discovered in a mountain in Toyonaka City and moved to its current location in [[1991]] (Heisei 3). * Shrine Office * Rest Area - Once an observation deck, it now offers limited views due to tree growth. * Shijonawate Shrine Hall * Off-site ** Small Kusunoki Imperial Mausoleum (Osaka Prefecture designated historic site) - The tomb of Masatsura Kusunoki, featuring a designated camphor tree. ** Tomb of Yoshihide Wada (Osaka Prefecture designated historic site) == Cultural Properties == === Important Cultural Property === * Sword signed "Kanei" - [[Kamakura period]], deposited in the Osaka History Museum. === Osaka Prefecture Designated Historic Sites === * Tomb of Masatsura Kusunoki * Tomb of Yoshihide Wada === Osaka Prefecture Designated Natural Monument === * Camphor tree at the tomb of Masatsura Kusunoki == festivals == * Spring Grand festival on April 5 * Autumn Grand festival on October 5 == Pilgrimage Sites == ; [[Kenmu Chuko Fifteen Shrines]] ; [[Naniwa Seven Fortunes Pilgrimage]] ; [[Sacred Sites of the Kinki Region Pilgrimage]] : 60 [[Hiraoka Shrine]] - '''61 Shijonawate Shrine''' - 62 [[Minase Shrine]] == Transportation == * 15-minute walk from [[Shijonawate Station]] on the [[Katamachi Line]] (Gakkentoshi Line) of [[West Japan Railway Company]] == References == {{Reflist}} == Related Books == *[https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/1112175/1/1 Yamada Yonekichi, "History of the Fifteen Shrines of Imperial Restoration," compiled by Nirokushinsho, 1935, pp. 82-85. (National Diet Library Digital Collections)] * Anzu Motohiko and Umeda Yoshihiko (editors and supervisors), "Shinto Dictionary," [[Shinto Newspaper Company]], 1968, p. 32. * [[Eiji Shirai]] and Toki Masanori (editors), "Shrine Dictionary," [[Tokyo-do Publishing]], 1979, pp. 163-164. == Related Articles == * [[Small Kusunoki Imperial Mausoleum]] - Karasu Minami-cho. * Tomb of [[Yoshihide Wada]] - Minamino 4-chome. * [[Sakurai Station Ruins]] * [[Battle of Shijonawate]] * [[Nanko Katsura]] - A rakugo storyteller living in Shijonawate City who was influenced by the Kusunoki story when he took his name. {{Commonscat|Shijonawate Jinja}} {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]s in Osaka}} {{Osaka Green 100 Selection}} {{Normdaten}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Shijonawate Shrine}} revision.text:
{{神社 |名称 = 四條畷神社 |画像 = [[Image:Shijonawate Jinja Honden 2018.jpg|250px]]
拝殿 |所在地 = [[大阪府]][[四條畷市]][[南野 (四条畷市)|南野]]2丁目18-1 |ISO = JP-27 |位置 = {{coord|34|43|56.7|N|135|38|57|E|region:JP-27|display=it}} |祭神 = [[楠木正行]] 他24柱| |社格 = [[別格官幣社]]
[[別表神社]]| |創建 = [[1890年]]（[[明治]]23年）| |本殿 = |別名 = |札所等 = [[建武中興十五社]]
[[なにわ七幸めぐり]]
[[神仏霊場巡拝の道]]第61番（大阪第20番）| |例祭 = 春季大祭・4月5日、秋季大祭・10月5日| |神事 = |地図 = {{Maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=205|frame-height=200|marker=religious-shinto|type2=shape|fill2=#f5fffa|stroke-color2=#006400|stroke-width2=1|frame-latitude=34.732|frame-longitude=135.649}} {{Location map|Japan Osaka|float=center|width=205|label=四條畷神社|position=left|caption=}} }} '''四條畷神社'''（しじょうなわてじんじゃ）は、[[大阪府]][[四條畷市]]にある[[神社]]。[[近代社格制度|旧社格]]は[[別格官幣社]]で、現在は[[神社本庁]]の[[別表神社]]。[[建武中興十五社]]の一社。[[大阪みどりの百選]]に選ばれている。 [[南朝 (日本)|南朝]]の将として戦い、[[四條畷の戦い]]で敗死した[[楠木正行]]を主祭神としている。父の[[楠木正成]]が大楠公（だいなんこう）と呼ばれるのに対して、嫡男の楠木正行は小楠公（しょうなんこう）と呼ばれるが、地元四條畷市民や隣接する[[大東市]]民の間では単に「楠公さん」といえば正行や当社を指す場合が多い。[[1975年]]（[[昭和]]50年）には「楠公」という町名まで誕生し、他にも周辺に楠公と付く地名が複数誕生している。 境内やその周辺には[[サクラ|桜]]が多く植えられており、春は多くの花見客でにぎわう。 == 祭神 == * 主祭神 - [[楠木正行]] 他、楠木一族の将士24柱を配祀している。 * [[楠木正時]]、[[楠木正家]]、楠木正家子息 * [[和田賢秀]]、[[和田正朝]]、和田紀六左衛門、和田紀六左衛門子息、和田紀六左衛門子息 * [[大塚惟久]]、畠山與三職俊、畠山六郎、[[野田城 (河内国)|野田四郎、野田四郎子息、野田四郎子息]] * 金岸（某）、金岸（某）弟、関住良円、関住良円子息、[[西阿 (人名)|三輪西阿]]、三輪西阿子息 * 河邊石掬丸、譽田（某）、阿間了願、青屋刑部 == 歴史 == [[正平 (日本)|正平]]3年（[[1348年]]）1月、この地で[[四條畷の戦い]]が行われ、[[高師直]]・[[高師泰|師泰]]兄弟が率いる[[北朝 (日本)|北朝]]軍に[[南朝 (日本)|南朝]]軍は敗北し、[[楠木正行]]、[[楠木正時|正時]]兄弟が自害したのを始め、多くの楠木一族が戦死した。その後、正行が自害した[[河内国]][[讃良郡]]南野村[[小字|字]]雁屋に「楠塚」と呼ばれる正行の墓が作られ、ひっそりと菩提が弔われた。 しかし、[[明治]]時代になると明治政府によって南朝が正統とされ、正行の父である[[楠木正成]]が大楠公（だいなんこう）として神格化されると、その父の遺志を継いで南朝のために戦い命を落とした嫡男の正行も小楠公（しょうなんこう）と呼ばれ、崇められるようになった。それに伴い、[[1878年]]（明治11年）に楠塚は「[[小楠公御墓所]]」と改められて整備され、その規模が拡大された。 同じ頃、南野村[[飯盛山 (生駒山地)|飯盛山]]の山麓にある住吉平田神社の神職らが中心となり、[[楠木氏]]らを祀る神社の創建を政府に願い出た結果、[[1889年]]（明治22年）[[12月16日]]に神社創立と[[別格官幣社]]四條畷神社の社号の宣下が勅許され[{{NDLDC|2945191}} 明治22年12月17日付官報（第1942号）] 内務省告示第六十一号、翌[[1890年]]（明治23年）に住吉平田神社の南隣の地に創建された。 創建以降、[[1895年]]（明治28年）に[[浪速鉄道]]が[[大阪市]]中心部から当社近くの[[四条畷駅]]まで開業するなどして四條畷神社周辺は大いに栄えることとなり、所在地の讃良郡甲可村（南野村ほか6ヶ村合併による）には「四條畷」と付く施設が次第に増え、「四條畷」は甲可村の別称のようになっていった。[[1932年]]（[[昭和]]7年）に甲可村はついに[[四條畷市|四條畷村]]に改称するに至り、これが現市名にまで継承されている。同年には浪速鉄道から転じて国鉄路線となっていた[[片町線]]の四条畷駅以西において関西国鉄初の電車の運転が開始されるなどして、四條畷神社創建をきっかけに周辺一帯は北河内地方随一の大市街地に発展し、その賑わいは現在も続いている。 [[1948年]]（昭和23年）に[[神社本庁]]の[[別表神社]]に加列されている。 当社の西には小楠公御墓所（大阪府指定[[史跡]]）が、北西には[[和田賢秀]]の墓（大阪府指定史跡）がある。
[[皇族]]・[[王公族]]の参拝歴

== 境内 == * 本殿 * 拝殿 * [[楠木正成]]・[[楠木正行|正行]]父子の「桜井の別れ」の像 - 忠孝両全と書かれている。 * 御妣（みおや）神社 - 祭神：久子刀自命（正行の母・久子）。摂社。 * 拝所 * 正行・久子母子の像 - 貞仁両全と書かれている。 * 鳥居 - [[1990年]]（[[平成]]2年）に創建100周年記念として[[伊勢神宮]]から贈られた。 * 楠天神社 - 祭神：[[菅原道真]] * 有源招魂社 - 祭神：内外古今の先哲先人の御霊。もともとは[[飯盛山 (生駒山地)|飯盛山]]の東にある有源山にあった成人教学研修所に祀られていたが、[[2007年]]（平成19年）4月7日に現在地に祭神を移して建立された。 * 楠公慰霊塔 - 楠木正成の供養塔。[[五輪塔]]の形式ではあるが、丸い水輪の石が二つある変わった形をしている。[[大阪府]][[豊中市]]の山中で発見された古い塔で、[[1991年]]（平成3年）に現在地に移された。 * 社務所 * 休憩所 - かつては展望台であったが、現在は木々が育って展望はあまり良くない。 * 四條畷神社会館 * 境外 ** [[小楠公御墓所]]（大阪府指定[[史跡]]） - 楠木正行の墓のほか、大阪府指定[[天然記念物]]の[[クスノキ|くす]]がある。 ** [[和田賢秀]]の墓（大阪府指定史跡） == 文化財 == === 重要文化財 === * 太刀 銘：包永 - [[鎌倉時代]]。[[大阪歴史博物館]]寄託。 === 大阪府指定史跡 === * 伝楠木正行墓 * 伝和田賢秀墓 === 大阪府指定天然記念物 === * 四條畷楠木正行墓のくす == 例祭 == * 春季大祭・4月5日、秋季大祭・10月5日 == 前後の札所 == ; [[建武中興十五社]] ; [[なにわ七幸めぐり]] ; [[神仏霊場巡拝の道]] : 60 [[枚岡神社]]　-　'''61 四條畷神社'''　-　62 [[水無瀬神宮]] == 交通アクセス == * [[西日本旅客鉄道|JR西日本]][[片町線]]（学研都市線） [[四条畷駅]]から徒歩15分 == 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}} == 関連図書 == *{{Cite book |和書 |title=別格官幣社四条畷神社社誌 補訂 |publisher=四条畷神社々務所|year=1938|id={{NDLJP|1098022}}|ref={{sfnRef|別格官幣社四条畷神社社誌 補訂|1938}}}} *[https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/1112175/1/1 二六興信所編纂　山田米吉編『勤王事蹟別格官幣社精史』82〜85頁　二六興信所　1935年（国立国会図書館デジタルコレクション）] * 安津素彦・梅田義彦編集兼監修者『神道辞典』[[神社新報]]社、1968年、32頁 * [[白井永二]]・土岐昌訓編集『神社辞典』[[東京堂出版]]、1979年、163-164頁 == 関連項目 == * [[小楠公御墓所]] - 雁屋南町。 * [[和田賢秀|和田賢秀墓]] - 南野4丁目。 * [[桜井駅跡]] * [[四條畷の戦い]] * [[桂南光 (3代目)]] - 当地四條畷市在住の落語家。襲名の際、楠公のことが影響したという {{Shinto-stub}} {{Commonscat|Shijonawate Jinja}} {{神道 横}} {{大阪みどりの百選}} {{Normdaten}} {{DEFAULTSORT:ししようなわてしんしや}} [[Category:四條畷神社|!]] [[Category:大阪府の神社]] [[Category:別格官幣社]] [[Category:別表神社]] [[Category:建武中興十五社]] [[Category:楠木氏|社ししようなわてしんしや]] [[Category:四條畷市の建築物]] [[Category:四條畷市の歴史]]

{{Short description|Shinto shrine located in Shijōnawate, Osaka Prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = {{nihongo|'''Shijōnawate Shrine'''|四條畷神社}} | image = Shijonawate Jinja Honden 2018.jpg | caption = Shijōnawate Jinja [[Honden]] | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34.73245|135.64917|type:landmark_region:JP|format=dms|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} '''Shijōnawate Shrine''' (四條畷神社, ''Shijōnawate jinja'') is a [[Shinto]] shrine located in [[Shijōnawate]], [[Osaka Prefecture]], Japan. Its main festival is held annually on February 12. It was founded in 1890, and enshrines [[Kusunoki Masatsura]] along with 24 other [[kami]]. It is one of the [[Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]]. ==Overview== {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Shijonawate Shrine | image = [[image:Shijonawate Jinja Honden 2018.jpg|250px]]
Worship Hall | location = Minamino 2-18-1, [[Shijonawate]], [[Osaka Prefecture]] | coordinates = {{coord|34|43|56.7|N|135|38|57|E|region:JP-27|display=it}} | deity = [[Masatsura Kusunoki]] and 24 others | Shrine rank = [[Bekkaku Kanpeisha]]
[[Beppyo Shrine]] | established = [[1890]] (Meiji 23) | main hall = | alternate names = | Pilgrimage = [[Kenmu Chuko Fifteen Shrines]]
[[Naniwa Seven Fortunes Pilgrimage]]
[[Sacred Sites of the Kinki Region Pilgrimage]] No. 61 (Osaka No. 20) | festival = Spring Grand festival on April 5, Autumn Grand festival on October 5 | ceremony = | map = {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=205|frame-height=200|marker=religious-shinto|type2=shape|fill2=#f5fffa|stroke-color2=#006400|stroke-width2=1|frame-latitude=34.732|frame-longitude=135.649}} {{location map|Japan Osaka Prefecture|float=center|width=205|label=Shijonawate Shrine|position=left|caption=}} }} '''Shijonawate Shrine''' (四條畷神社, Shijōnawate Jinja) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Shijonawate]], [[Osaka Prefecture]]. The shrine was formerly ranked as a [[Bekkaku Kanpeisha]] and is currently listed as a [[Beppyo Shrine]] under the jurisdiction of the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]]. It is one of the [[Kenmu Chuko Fifteen Shrines]] and is selected as one of the [[100 Famous Green Sites of Osaka]]. The shrine enshrines [[Masatsura Kusunoki]], who fought as a commander for the [[Southern Court (Japan)|Southern Court]] and died in the [[Battle of Shijonawate]]. While his father, [[Masashige Kusunoki]], is known as the "Great Kusunoki" (大楠公, Dai Nankō), his eldest son, Masatsura, is known as the "Small Kusunoki" (小楠公, Shō Nankō). Locally, the term "Kusunoki-san" often refers to Masatsura or the shrine itself. In [[1975]] (Showa 50), a town named "Kusunoki" was established, and several other places in the vicinity are named after him. The shrine grounds and surrounding areas are known for their cherry blossoms, attracting many visitors during the spring. == deity == * Main deity - [[Masatsura Kusunoki]] Additionally, 24 other warriors of the Kusunoki clan are enshrined: * [[Masatoki Kusunoki]], [[Masaie Kusunoki]], sons of Masaie Kusunoki * [[Yoshihide Wada]], [[Masatomo Wada]], Wada Kirokuzaemon, sons of Wada Kirokuzaemon * [[Iku Hisahide]], Hatakeyama Yosanosuke Toshiaki, Hatakeyama Rokurō, [[Noda Castle (Kawachi Province)|Noda Shiro]], sons of Noda Shiro * Kane no Shō (某), younger brother of Kane no Shō, Seki Jū Ryōen, son of Seki Jū Ryōen, [[Nishi A (name)|Miwa Nishi A]], son of Miwa Nishi A * Kawabe Ishikimaru, Hōda (某), Amaryōgan, Aoya Gyōbu == History == In [[1348]] (Shōhei 3), the [[Battle of Shijonawate]] took place here, resulting in the defeat of the Southern Court's forces by the Northern Court's army led by the brothers [[Tadayoshi Ashikaga|Tadayoshi]] and [[Takauji Ashikaga|Takauji]]. Masatsura Kusunoki and his brother [[Masatoki Kusunoki|Masatoki]] committed suicide, along with many others from the Kusunoki clan. Afterward, a tomb called "Kusunotsuka" was built for Masatsura in Nanino Village, and his spirit was quietly enshrined. In the [[Meiji era]], the government recognized the Southern Court as the legitimate ruling entity, leading to the deification of Masatsura's father, [[Masashige Kusunoki]], as the "Great Kusunoki" (大楠公). Masatsura, who followed his father's will and fought for the Southern Court, was also revered as the "Small Kusunoki" (小楠公). In [[1878]] (Meiji 11), his tomb was renamed "Small Kusunoki Imperial Mausoleum" and expanded. Around the same time, officials from Sumiyoshi Hirata Shrine near Mount Iimori petitioned the government to establish a shrine for the Kusunoki clan. The petition was granted, and on December 16, [[1889]] (Meiji 22), the founding of Shijonawate Shrine and its designation as a [[Bekkaku Kanpeisha]] were decreed[{{NDLDC|2945191}} Meiji 22 December 17 Official Gazette (No. 1942)] Ministry of the Interior Notice No. 61. The shrine was established in [[1890]] (Meiji 23) next to Sumiyoshi Hirata Shrine. The area around the shrine flourished, particularly after the Naniwa Railway opened a line from central [[Osaka]] to nearby Shijonawate Station in [[1895]] (Meiji 28). The name "Shijonawate" became associated with the area, and in [[1932]] (Showa 7), the village of Koka was renamed Shijonawate Village, later becoming Shijonawate City. The surrounding region developed into one of the largest urban areas in northern [[Kawachi Province]], a status it maintains today. In [[1948]] (Showa 23), the shrine was included in the list of [[Beppyo Shrines]] by the Association of Shinto Shrines. To the west of the shrine lies the Small Kusunoki Imperial Mausoleum (an Osaka Prefecture designated historic site), and to the northwest is the tomb of [[Yoshihide Wada]] (also a designated historic site). == Shrine Grounds == * main hall * Worship Hall * Statue of Masashige and Masatsura Kusunoki, depicting their farewell at Sakurai - Inscribed with "Loyalty and Filial Piety." * Miboyashi Shrine - Enshrines Kusunoki's mother, Kusako Taji no Mikoto. Sub-shrine. * Worship Place * Statue of Masatsura and his mother Kusako - Inscribed with "Loyalty and Filial Piety." * Torii - Gifted from Ise Grand Shrine in [[1990]] (Heisei 2) to commemorate the shrine's 100th anniversary. * Kusunoki Tenjin Shrine - Enshrines [[Sugawara no Michizane]] * Yugen Reikonsha - Enshrines the spirits of ancient and modern scholars and leaders. Originally enshrined at the Adult Education Training Center on the eastern side of Mount Iimori, it was relocated to the current site on April 7, [[2007]] (Heisei 19). * Kusunoki Memorial Tower - A memorial tower for Masashige Kusunoki. It has an unusual design with two round stones representing the water element, discovered in a mountain in Toyonaka City and moved to its current location in [[1991]] (Heisei 3). * Shrine Office * Rest Area - Once an observation deck, it now offers limited views due to tree growth. * Shijonawate Shrine Hall * Off-site ** Small Kusunoki Imperial Mausoleum (Osaka Prefecture designated historic site) - The tomb of Masatsura Kusunoki, featuring a designated camphor tree. ** Tomb of Yoshihide Wada (Osaka Prefecture designated historic site) == Cultural Properties == === Important Cultural Property === * Sword signed "Kanei" - [[Kamakura period]], deposited in the Osaka History Museum. === Osaka Prefecture Designated Historic Sites === * Tomb of Masatsura Kusunoki * Tomb of Yoshihide Wada === Osaka Prefecture Designated Natural Monument === * Camphor tree at the tomb of Masatsura Kusunoki == festivals == * Spring Grand festival on April 5 * Autumn Grand festival on October 5 == Pilgrimage Sites == ; [[Kenmu Chuko Fifteen Shrines]] ; [[Naniwa Seven Fortunes Pilgrimage]] ; [[Sacred Sites of the Kinki Region Pilgrimage]] : 60 [[Hiraoka Shrine]] - '''61 Shijonawate Shrine''' - 62 [[Minase Shrine]] == Transportation == * 15-minute walk from [[Shijonawate Station]] on the [[Katamachi Line]] (Gakkentoshi Line) of [[West Japan Railway Company]] == References == {{Reflist}} == Related Books == *[https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/1112175/1/1 Yamada Yonekichi, "History of the Fifteen Shrines of Imperial Restoration," compiled by Nirokushinsho, 1935, pp. 82-85. (National Diet Library Digital Collections)] * Anzu Motohiko and Umeda Yoshihiko (editors and supervisors), "Shinto Dictionary," [[Shinto Newspaper Company]], 1968, p. 32. * [[Eiji Shirai]] and Toki Masanori (editors), "Shrine Dictionary," [[Tokyo-do Publishing]], 1979, pp. 163-164. == Related Articles == * [[Small Kusunoki Imperial Mausoleum]] - Karasu Minami-cho. * Tomb of [[Yoshihide Wada]] - Minamino 4-chome. * [[Sakurai Station Ruins]] * [[Battle of Shijonawate]] * [[Nanko Katsura]] - A rakugo storyteller living in Shijonawate City who was influenced by the Kusunoki story when he took {{Short description|Shinto shrine located in Shijōnawate, Osaka Prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = {{nihongo|'''Shijōnawate Shrine'''|四條畷神社}} | image = Shijonawate Jinja Honden 2018.jpg | caption = Shijōnawate Jinja [[Honden]] | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34.73245|135.64917|type:landmark_region:JP|format=dms|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} '''Shijōnawate Shrine''' (四條畷神社, ''Shijōnawate jinja'') is a [[Shinto]] shrine located in [[Shijōnawate]], [[Osaka Prefecture]], Japan. Its main festival is held annually on February 12. It was founded in 1890, and enshrines [[Kusunoki Masatsura]] along with 24 other [[kami]]. It is one of the [[Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]]. ==Overview== {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Shijonawate Shrine | image = [[image:Shijonawate Jinja Honden 2018.jpg|250px]]
Worship Hall | location = Minamino 2-18-1, [[Shijonawate]], [[Osaka Prefecture]] | coordinates = {{coord|34|43|56.7|N|135|38|57|E|region:JP-27|display=it}} | deity = [[Masatsura Kusunoki]] and 24 others | Shrine rank = [[Bekkaku Kanpeisha]]
[[Beppyo Shrine]] | established = [[1890]] (Meiji 23) | main hall = | alternate names = | Pilgrimage = [[Kenmu Chuko Fifteen Shrines]]
[[Naniwa Seven Fortunes Pilgrimage]]
[[Sacred Sites of the Kinki Region Pilgrimage]] No. 61 (Osaka No. 20) | festival = Spring Grand festival on April 5, Autumn Grand festival on October 5 | ceremony = | map = {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=205|frame-height=200|marker=religious-shinto|type2=shape|fill2=#f5fffa|stroke-color2=#006400|stroke-width2=1|frame-latitude=34.732|frame-longitude=135.649}} {{location map|Japan Osaka Prefecture|float=center|width=205|label=Shijonawate Shrine|position=left|caption=}} }} '''Shijonawate Shrine''' (四條畷神社, Shijōnawate Jinja) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Shijonawate]], [[Osaka Prefecture]]. The shrine was formerly ranked as a [[Bekkaku Kanpeisha]] and is currently listed as a [[Beppyo Shrine]] under the jurisdiction of the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]]. It is one of the [[Kenmu Chuko Fifteen Shrines]] and is selected as one of the [[100 Famous Green Sites of Osaka]]. The shrine enshrines [[Masatsura Kusunoki]], who fought as a commander for the [[Southern Court (Japan)|Southern Court]] and died in the [[Battle of Shijonawate]]. While his father, [[Masashige Kusunoki]], is known as the "Great Kusunoki" (大楠公, Dai Nankō), his eldest son, Masatsura, is known as the "Small Kusunoki" (小楠公, Shō Nankō). Locally, the term "Kusunoki-san" often refers to Masatsura or the shrine itself. In [[1975]] (Showa 50), a town named "Kusunoki" was established, and several other places in the vicinity are named after him. The shrine grounds and surrounding areas are known for their cherry blossoms, attracting many visitors during the spring. == deity == * Main deity - [[Masatsura Kusunoki]] Additionally, 24 other warriors of the Kusunoki clan are enshrined: * [[Masatoki Kusunoki]], [[Masaie Kusunoki]], sons of Masaie Kusunoki * [[Yoshihide Wada]], [[Masatomo Wada]], Wada Kirokuzaemon, sons of Wada Kirokuzaemon * [[Iku Hisahide]], Hatakeyama Yosanosuke Toshiaki, Hatakeyama Rokurō, [[Noda Castle (Kawachi Province)|Noda Shiro]], sons of Noda Shiro * Kane no Shō (某), younger brother of Kane no Shō, Seki Jū Ryōen, son of Seki Jū Ryōen, [[Nishi A (name)|Miwa Nishi A]], son of Miwa Nishi A * Kawabe Ishikimaru, Hōda (某), Amaryōgan, Aoya Gyōbu == History == In [[1348]] (Shōhei 3), the [[Battle of Shijonawate]] took place here, resulting in the defeat of the Southern Court's forces by the Northern Court's army led by the brothers [[Tadayoshi Ashikaga|Tadayoshi]] and [[Takauji Ashikaga|Takauji]]. Masatsura Kusunoki and his brother [[Masatoki Kusunoki|Masatoki]] committed suicide, along with many others from the Kusunoki clan. Afterward, a tomb called "Kusunotsuka" was built for Masatsura in Nanino Village, and his spirit was quietly enshrined. In the [[Meiji era]], the government recognized the Southern Court as the legitimate ruling entity, leading to the deification of Masatsura's father, [[Masashige Kusunoki]], as the "Great Kusunoki" (大楠公). Masatsura, who followed his father's will and fought for the Southern Court, was also revered as the "Small Kusunoki" (小楠公). In [[1878]] (Meiji 11), his tomb was renamed "Small Kusunoki Imperial Mausoleum" and expanded. Around the same time, officials from Sumiyoshi Hirata Shrine near Mount Iimori petitioned the government to establish a shrine for the Kusunoki clan. The petition was granted, and on December 16, [[1889]] (Meiji 22), the founding of Shijonawate Shrine and its designation as a [[Bekkaku Kanpeisha]] were decreed[{{NDLDC|2945191}} Meiji 22 December 17 Official Gazette (No. 1942)] Ministry of the Interior Notice No. 61. The shrine was established in [[1890]] (Meiji 23) next to Sumiyoshi Hirata Shrine. The area around the shrine flourished, particularly after the Naniwa Railway opened a line from central [[Osaka]] to nearby Shijonawate Station in [[1895]] (Meiji 28). The name "Shijonawate" became associated with the area, and in [[1932]] (Showa 7), the village of Koka was renamed Shijonawate Village, later becoming Shijonawate City. The surrounding region developed into one of the largest urban areas in northern [[Kawachi Province]], a status it maintains today. In [[1948]] (Showa 23), the shrine was included in the list of [[Beppyo Shrines]] by the Association of Shinto Shrines. To the west of the shrine lies the Small Kusunoki Imperial Mausoleum (an Osaka Prefecture designated historic site), and to the northwest is the tomb of [[Yoshihide Wada]] (also a designated historic site). == Shrine Grounds == * main hall * Worship Hall * Statue of Masashige and Masatsura Kusunoki, depicting their farewell at Sakurai - Inscribed with "Loyalty and Filial Piety." * Miboyashi Shrine - Enshrines Kusunoki's mother, Kusako Taji no Mikoto. Sub-shrine. * Worship Place * Statue of Masatsura and his mother Kusako - Inscribed with "Loyalty and Filial Piety." * Torii - Gifted from Ise Grand Shrine in [[1990]] (Heisei 2) to commemorate the shrine's 100th anniversary. * Kusunoki Tenjin Shrine - Enshrines [[Sugawara no Michizane]] * Yugen Reikonsha - Enshrines the spirits of ancient and modern scholars and leaders. Originally enshrined at the Adult Education Training Center on the eastern side of Mount Iimori, it was relocated to the current site on April 7, [[2007]] (Heisei 19). * Kusunoki Memorial Tower - A memorial tower for Masashige Kusunoki. It has an unusual design with two round stones representing the water element, discovered in a mountain in Toyonaka City and moved to its current location in [[1991]] (Heisei 3). * Shrine Office * Rest Area - Once an observation deck, it now offers limited views due to tree growth. * Shijonawate Shrine Hall * Off-site ** Small Kusunoki Imperial Mausoleum (Osaka Prefecture designated historic site) - The tomb of Masatsura Kusunoki, featuring a designated camphor tree. ** Tomb of Yoshihide Wada (Osaka Prefecture designated historic site) == Cultural Properties == === Important Cultural Property === * Sword signed "Kanei" - [[Kamakura period]], deposited in the Osaka History Museum. === Osaka Prefecture Designated Historic Sites === * Tomb of Masatsura Kusunoki * Tomb of Yoshihide Wada === Osaka Prefecture Designated Natural Monument === * Camphor tree at the tomb of Masatsura Kusunoki == festivals == * Spring Grand festival on April 5 * Autumn Grand festival on October 5 == Pilgrimage Sites == ; [[Kenmu Chuko Fifteen Shrines]] ; [[Naniwa Seven Fortunes Pilgrimage]] ; [[Sacred Sites of the Kinki Region Pilgrimage]] : 60 [[Hiraoka Shrine]] - '''61 Shijonawate Shrine''' - 62 [[Minase Shrine]] == Transportation == * 15-minute walk from [[Shijonawate Station]] on the [[Katamachi Line]] (Gakkentoshi Line) of [[West Japan Railway Company]] == References == {{Reflist}} == Related Books == *[https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/1112175/1/1 Yamada Yonekichi, "History of the Fifteen Shrines of Imperial Restoration," compiled by Nirokushinsho, 1935, pp. 82-85. (National Diet Library Digital Collections)] * Anzu Motohiko and Umeda Yoshihiko (editors and supervisors), "Shinto Dictionary," [[Shinto Newspaper Company]], 1968, p. 32. * [[Eiji Shirai]] and Toki Masanori (editors), "Shrine Dictionary," [[Tokyo-do Publishing]], 1979, pp. 163-164. == Related Articles == * [[Small Kusunoki Imperial Mausoleum]] - Karasu Minami-cho. * Tomb of [[Yoshihide Wada]] - Minamino 4-chome. * [[Sakurai Station Ruins]] * [[Battle of Shijonawate]] * [[Nanko Katsura]] - A rakugo storyteller living in Shijonawate City who was influenced by the Kusunoki story when he took his name. {{Commonscat|Shijonawate Jinja}} {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]s in Osaka}} {{Osaka Green 100 Selection}} {{Normdaten}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Shijonawate Shrine}} [[Sacred Sites of the Kinki Region Pilgrimage]] No. 61 (Osaka No. 20) | festival = Spring Grand festival on April 5, Autumn Grand festival on October 5 | ceremony = | map = {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=205|frame-height=200|marker=religious-shinto|type2=shape|fill2=#f5fffa|stroke-color2=#006400|stroke-width2=1|frame-latitude=34.732|frame-longitude=135.649}} {{location map|Japan Osaka Prefecture|float=center|width=205|label=Shijonawate Shrine|position=left|caption=}} }} '''Shijonawate Shrine''' (四條畷神社, Shijōnawate Jinja) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Shijonawate]], [[Osaka Prefecture]]. The shrine was formerly ranked as a [[Bekkaku Kanpeisha]] and is currently listed as a [[Beppyo Shrine]] under the jurisdiction of the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]]. It is one of the [[Kenmu Chuko Fifteen Shrines]] and is selected as one of the [[100 Famous Green Sites of Osaka]]. The shrine enshrines [[Masatsura Kusunoki]], who fought as a commander for the [[Southern Court (Japan)|Southern Court]] and died in the [[Battle of Shijonawate]]. While his father, [[Masashige Kusunoki]], is known as the "Great Kusunoki" (大楠公, Dai Nankō), his eldest son, Masatsura, is known as the "Small Kusunoki" (小楠公, Shō Nankō). Locally, the term "Kusunoki-san" often refers to Masatsura or the shrine itself. In [[1975]] (Showa 50), a town named "Kusunoki" was established, and several other places in the vicinity are named after him. The shrine grounds and surrounding areas are known for their cherry blossoms, attracting many visitors during the spring. == deity == * Main deity - [[Masatsura Kusunoki]] Additionally, 24 other warriors of the Kusunoki clan are enshrined: * [[Masatoki Kusunoki]], [[Masaie Kusunoki]], sons of Masaie Kusunoki * [[Yoshihide Wada]], [[Masatomo Wada]], Wada Kirokuzaemon, sons of Wada Kirokuzaemon * [[Iku Hisahide]], Hatakeyama Yosanosuke Toshiaki, Hatakeyama Rokurō, [[Noda Castle (Kawachi Province)|Noda Shiro]], sons of Noda Shiro * Kane no Shō (某), younger brother of Kane no Shō, Seki Jū Ryōen, son of Seki Jū Ryōen, [[Nishi A (name)|Miwa Nishi A]], son of Miwa Nishi A * Kawabe Ishikimaru, Hōda (某), Amaryōgan, Aoya Gyōbu == History == In [[1348]] (Shōhei 3), the [[Battle of Shijonawate]] took place here, resulting in the defeat of the Southern Court's forces by the Northern Court's army led by the brothers [[Tadayoshi Ashikaga|Tadayoshi]] and [[Takauji Ashikaga|Takauji]]. Masatsura Kusunoki and his brother [[Masatoki Kusunoki|Masatoki]] committed suicide, along with many others from the Kusunoki clan. Afterward, a tomb called "Kusunotsuka" was built for Masatsura in Nanino Village, and his spirit was quietly enshrined. In the [[Meiji era]], the government recognized the Southern Court as the legitimate ruling entity, leading to the deification of Masatsura's father, [[Masashige Kusunoki]], as the "Great Kusunoki" (大楠公). Masatsura, who followed his father's will and fought for the Southern Court, was also revered as the "Small Kusunoki" (小楠公). In [[1878]] (Meiji 11), his tomb was renamed "Small Kusunoki Imperial Mausoleum" and expanded. Around the same time, officials from Sumiyoshi Hirata Shrine near Mount Iimori petitioned the government to establish a shrine for the Kusunoki clan. The petition was granted, and on December 16, [[1889]] (Meiji 22), the founding of Shijonawate Shrine and its designation as a [[Bekkaku Kanpeisha]] were decreed[{{NDLDC|2945191}} Meiji 22 December 17 Official Gazette (No. 1942)] Ministry of the Interior Notice No. 61. The shrine was established in [[1890]] (Meiji 23) next to Sumiyoshi Hirata Shrine. The area around the shrine flourished, particularly after the Naniwa Railway opened a line from central [[Osaka]] to nearby Shijonawate Station in [[1895]] (Meiji 28). The name "Shijonawate" became associated with the area, and in [[1932]] (Showa 7), the village of Koka was renamed Shijonawate Village, later becoming Shijonawate City. The surrounding region developed into one of the largest urban areas in northern [[Kawachi Province]], a status it maintains today. In [[1948]] (Showa 23), the shrine was included in the list of [[Beppyo Shrines]] by the Association of Shinto Shrines. To the west of the shrine lies the Small Kusunoki Imperial Mausoleum (an Osaka Prefecture designated historic site), and to the northwest is the tomb of [[Yoshihide Wada]] (also a designated historic site). == Shrine Grounds == * main hall * Worship Hall * Statue of Masashige and Masatsura Kusunoki, depicting their farewell at Sakurai - Inscribed with "Loyalty and Filial Piety." * Miboyashi Shrine - Enshrines Kusunoki's mother, Kusako Taji no Mikoto. Sub-shrine. * Worship Place * Statue of Masatsura and his mother Kusako - Inscribed with "Loyalty and Filial Piety." * Torii - Gifted from Ise Grand Shrine in [[1990]] (Heisei 2) to commemorate the shrine's 100th anniversary. * Kusunoki Tenjin Shrine - Enshrines [[Sugawara no Michizane]] * Yugen Reikonsha - Enshrines the spirits of ancient and modern scholars and leaders. Originally enshrined at the Adult Education Training Center on the eastern side of Mount Iimori, it was relocated to the current site on April 7, [[2007]] (Heisei 19). * Kusunoki Memorial Tower - A memorial tower for Masashige Kusunoki. It has an unusual design with two round stones representing the water element, discovered in a mountain in Toyonaka City and moved to its current location in [[1991]] (Heisei 3). * Shrine Office * Rest Area - Once an observation deck, it now offers limited views due to tree growth. * Shijonawate Shrine Hall * Off-site ** Small Kusunoki Imperial Mausoleum (Osaka Prefecture designated historic site) - The tomb of Masatsura Kusunoki, featuring a designated camphor tree. ** Tomb of Yoshihide Wada (Osaka Prefecture designated historic site) == Cultural Properties == === Important Cultural Property === * Sword signed "Kanei" - [[Kamakura period]], deposited in the Osaka History Museum. === Osaka Prefecture Designated Historic Sites === * Tomb of Masatsura Kusunoki * Tomb of Yoshihide Wada === Osaka Prefecture Designated Natural Monument === * Camphor tree at the tomb of Masatsura Kusunoki == festivals == * Spring Grand festival on April 5 * Autumn Grand festival on October 5 == Pilgrimage Sites == ; [[Kenmu Chuko Fifteen Shrines]] ; [[Naniwa Seven Fortunes Pilgrimage]] ; [[Sacred Sites of the Kinki Region Pilgrimage]] : 60 [[Hiraoka Shrine]] - '''61 Shijonawate Shrine''' - 62 [[Minase Shrine]] == Transportation == * 15-minute walk from [[Shijonawate Station]] on the [[Katamachi Line]] (Gakkentoshi Line) of [[West Japan Railway Company]] == References == {{Reflist}} == Related Books == *[https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/1112175/1/1 Yamada Yonekichi, "History of the Fifteen Shrines of Imperial Restoration," compiled by Nirokushinsho, 1935, pp. 82-85. (National Diet Library Digital Collections)] * Anzu Motohiko and Umeda Yoshihiko (editors and supervisors), "Shinto Dictionary," [[Shinto Newspaper Company]], 1968, p. 32. * [[Eiji Shirai]] and Toki Masanori (editors), "Shrine Dictionary," [[Tokyo-do Publishing]], 1979, pp. 163-164. == Related Articles == * [[Small Kusunoki Imperial Mausoleum]] - Karasu Minami-cho. * Tomb of [[Yoshihide Wada]] - Minamino 4-chome. * [[Sakurai Station Ruins]] * [[Battle of Shijonawate]] * [[Nanko Katsura]] - A rakugo storyteller living in Shijonawate City who was influenced by the Kusunoki story when he took The shrine grounds and surrounding areas are known for their cherry blossoms, attracting many visitors during the spring. == deity == * Main deity - [[Masatsura Kusunoki]] Additionally, 24 other warriors of the Kusunoki clan are enshrined: * [[Masatoki Kusunoki]], [[Masaie Kusunoki]], sons of Masaie Kusunoki * [[Yoshihide Wada]], [[Masatomo Wada]], Wada Kirokuzaemon, sons of Wada Kirokuzaemon * [[Iku Hisahide]], Hatakeyama Yosanosuke Toshiaki, Hatakeyama Rokurō, [[Noda Castle (Kawachi Province)|Noda Shiro]], sons of Noda Shiro * Kane no Shō (某), younger brother of Kane no Shō, Seki Jū Ryōen, son of Seki Jū Ryōen, [[Nishi A (name)|Miwa Nishi A]], son of Miwa Nishi A * Kawabe Ishikimaru, Hōda (某), Amaryōgan, Aoya Gyōbu == History == In [[1348]] (Shōhei 3), the [[Battle of Shijonawate]] took place here, resulting in the defeat of the Southern Court's forces by the Northern Court's army led by the brothers [[Tadayoshi Ashikaga|Tadayoshi]] and [[Takauji Ashikaga|Takauji]]. Masatsura Kusunoki and his brother [[Masatoki Kusunoki|Masatoki]] committed suicide, along with many others from the Kusunoki clan. Afterward, a tomb called "Kusunotsuka" was built for Masatsura in Nanino Village, and his spirit was quietly enshrined. In the [[Meiji era]], the government recognized the Southern Court as the legitimate ruling entity, leading to the deification of Masatsura's father, [[Masashige Kusunoki]], as the "Great Kusunoki" (大楠公). Masatsura, who followed his father's will and fought for the Southern Court, was also revered as the "Small Kusunoki" (小楠公). In [[1878]] (Meiji 11), his tomb was renamed "Small Kusunoki Imperial Mausoleum" and expanded. Around the same time, officials from Sumiyoshi Hirata Shrine near Mount Iimori petitioned the government to establish a shrine for the Kusunoki clan. The petition was granted, and on December 16, [[1889]] (Meiji 22), the founding of Shijonawate Shrine and its designation as a [[Bekkaku Kanpeisha]] were decreed[{{NDLDC|2945191}} Meiji 22 December 17 Official Gazette (No. 1942)] Ministry of the Interior Notice No. 61. The shrine was established in [[1890]] (Meiji 23) next to Sumiyoshi Hirata Shrine. The area around the shrine flourished, particularly after the Naniwa Railway opened a line from central [[Osaka]] to nearby Shijonawate Station in [[1895]] (Meiji 28). The name "Shijonawate" became associated with the area, and in [[1932]] (Showa 7), the village of Koka was renamed Shijonawate Village, later becoming Shijonawate City. The surrounding region developed into one of the largest urban areas in northern [[Kawachi Province]], a status it maintains today. In [[1948]] (Showa 23), the shrine was included in the list of [[Beppyo Shrines]] by the Association of Shinto Shrines. To the west of the shrine lies the Small Kusunoki Imperial Mausoleum (an Osaka Prefecture designated historic site), and to the northwest is the tomb of [[Yoshihide Wada]] (also a designated historic site). == Shrine Grounds == * main hall * Worship Hall * Statue of Masashige and Masatsura Kusunoki, depicting their farewell at Sakurai - Inscribed with "Loyalty and Filial Piety." * Miboyashi Shrine - Enshrines Kusunoki's mother, Kusako Taji no Mikoto. Sub-shrine. * Worship Place * Statue of Masatsura and his mother Kusako - Inscribed with "Loyalty and Filial Piety." * Torii - Gifted from Ise Grand Shrine in [[1990]] (Heisei 2) to commemorate the shrine's 100th anniversary. * Kusunoki Tenjin Shrine - Enshrines [[Sugawara no Michizane]] * Yugen Reikonsha - Enshrines the spirits of ancient and modern scholars and leaders. Originally enshrined at the Adult Education Training Center on the eastern side of Mount Iimori, it was relocated to the current site on April 7, [[2007]] (Heisei 19). * Kusunoki Memorial Tower - A memorial tower for Masashige Kusunoki. It has an unusual design with two round stones representing the water element, discovered in a mountain in Toyonaka City and moved to its current location in [[1991]] (Heisei 3). * Shrine Office * Rest Area - Once an observation deck, it now offers limited views due to tree growth. * Shijonawate Shrine Hall * Off-site ** Small Kusunoki Imperial Mausoleum (Osaka Prefecture designated historic site) - The tomb of Masatsura Kusunoki, featuring a designated camphor tree. ** Tomb of Yoshihide Wada (Osaka Prefecture designated historic site) == Cultural Properties == === Important Cultural Property === * Sword signed "Kanei" - [[Kamakura period]], deposited in the Osaka History Museum. === Osaka Prefecture Designated Historic Sites === * Tomb of Masatsura Kusunoki * Tomb of Yoshihide Wada === Osaka Prefecture Designated Natural Monument === * Camphor tree at the tomb of Masatsura Kusunoki == festivals == * Spring Grand festival on April 5 * Autumn Grand festival on October 5 == Pilgrimage Sites == ; [[Kenmu Chuko Fifteen Shrines]] ; [[Naniwa Seven Fortunes Pilgrimage]] ; [[Sacred Sites of the Kinki Region Pilgrimage]] : 60 [[Hiraoka Shrine]] - '''61 Shijonawate Shrine''' - 62 [[Minase Shrine]] == Transportation == * 15-minute walk from [[Shijonawate Station]] on the [[Katamachi Line]] (Gakkentoshi Line) of [[West Japan Railway Company]] == References == {{Reflist}} == Related Books == *[https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/1112175/1/1 Yamada Yonekichi, "History of the Fifteen Shrines of Imperial Restoration," compiled by Nirokushinsho, 1935, pp. 82-85. The petition was granted, and on December 16, [[1889]] (Meiji 22), the founding of Shijonawate Shrine and its designation as a [[Bekkaku Kanpeisha]] were decreed[{{NDLDC|2945191}} Meiji 22 December 17 Official Gazette (No. 1942)] Ministry of the Interior Notice No. 61. The shrine was established in [[1890]] (Meiji 23) next to Sumiyoshi Hirata Shrine. The area around the shrine flourished, particularly after the Naniwa Railway opened a line from central [[Osaka]] to nearby Shijonawate Station in [[1895]] (Meiji 28). The name "Shijonawate" became associated with the area, and in [[1932]] (Showa 7), the village of Koka was renamed Shijonawate Village, later becoming Shijonawate City. The surrounding region developed into one of the largest urban areas in northern [[Kawachi Province]], a status it maintains today. In [[1948]] (Showa 23), the shrine was included in the list of [[Beppyo Shrines]] by the Association of Shinto Shrines. To the west of the shrine lies the Small Kusunoki Imperial Mausoleum (an Osaka Prefecture designated historic site), and to the northwest is the tomb of [[Yoshihide Wada]] (also a designated historic site). == Shrine Grounds == * main hall * Worship Hall * Statue of Masashige and Masatsura Kusunoki, depicting their farewell at Sakurai - Inscribed with "Loyalty and Filial Piety." * Miboyashi Shrine - Enshrines Kusunoki's mother, Kusako Taji no Mikoto. Sub-shrine. * Worship Place * Statue of Masatsura and his mother Kusako - Inscribed with "Loyalty and Filial Piety." * Torii - Gifted from Ise Grand Shrine in [[1990]] (Heisei 2) to commemorate the shrine's 100th anniversary. * Kusunoki Tenjin Shrine - Enshrines [[Sugawara no Michizane]] * Yugen Reikonsha - Enshrines the spirits of ancient and modern scholars and leaders. Originally enshrined at the Adult Education Training Center on the eastern side of Mount Iimori, it was relocated to the current site on April 7, [[2007]] (Heisei 19). * Kusunoki Memorial Tower - A memorial tower for Masashige Kusunoki. It has an unusual design with two round stones representing the water element, discovered in a mountain in Toyonaka City and moved to its current location in [[1991]] (Heisei 3). * Shrine Office * Rest Area - Once an observation deck, it now offers limited views due to tree growth. * Shijonawate Shrine Hall * Off-site ** Small Kusunoki Imperial Mausoleum (Osaka Prefecture designated historic site) - The tomb of Masatsura Kusunoki, featuring a designated camphor tree. ** Tomb of Yoshihide Wada (Osaka Prefecture designated historic site) == Cultural Properties == === Important Cultural Property === * Sword signed "Kanei" - [[Kamakura period]], deposited in the Osaka History Museum. === Osaka Prefecture Designated Historic Sites === * Tomb of Masatsura Kusunoki * Tomb of Yoshihide Wada === Osaka Prefecture Designated Natural Monument === * Camphor tree at the tomb of Masatsura Kusunoki == festivals == * Spring Grand festival on April 5 * Autumn Grand festival on October 5 == Pilgrimage Sites == ; [[Kenmu Chuko Fifteen Shrines]] ; [[Naniwa Seven Fortunes Pilgrimage]] ; [[Sacred Sites of the Kinki Region Pilgrimage]] : 60 [[Hiraoka Shrine]] - '''61 Shijonawate Shrine''' - 62 [[Minase Shrine]] == Transportation == * 15-minute walk from [[Shijonawate Station]] on the [[Katamachi Line]] (Gakkentoshi Line) of [[West Japan Railway Company]] == References == {{Reflist}} == Related Books == *[https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/1112175/1/1 Yamada Yonekichi, "History of the Fifteen Shrines of Imperial Restoration," compiled by Nirokushinsho, 1935, pp. 82-85. {{Short description|Shinto shrine located in Shijōnawate, Osaka Prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = {{nihongo|'''Shijōnawate Shrine'''|四條畷神社}} | image = Shijonawate Jinja Honden 2018.jpg | caption = Shijōnawate Jinja [[Honden]] | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34.73245|135.64917|type:landmark_region:JP|format=dms|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} '''Shijōnawate Shrine''' (四條畷神社, ''Shijōnawate jinja'') is a [[Shinto]] shrine located in [[Shijōnawate]], [[Osaka Prefecture]], Japan. Its main festival is held annually on February 12. It was founded in 1890, and enshrines [[Kusunoki Masatsura]] along with 24 other [[kami]]. It is one of the [[Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]]. ==Overview== {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Shijonawate Shrine | image = [[image:Shijonawate Jinja Honden 2018.jpg|250px]]
Worship Hall | location = Minamino 2-18-1, [[Shijonawate]], [[Osaka Prefecture]] | coordinates = {{coord|34|43|56.7|N|135|38|57|E|region:JP-27|display=it}} | deity = [[Masatsura Kusunoki]] and 24 others | Shrine rank = [[Bekkaku Kanpeisha]]
[[Beppyo Shrine]] | established = [[1890]] (Meiji 23) | main hall = | alternate names = | Pilgrimage = [[Kenmu Chuko Fifteen Shrines]]
[[Naniwa Seven Fortunes Pilgrimage]]
[[Sacred Sites of the Kinki Region Pilgrimage]] No. 61 (Osaka No. 20) | festival = Spring Grand festival on April 5, Autumn Grand festival on October 5 | ceremony = | map = {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=205|frame-height=200|marker=religious-shinto|type2=shape|fill2=#f5fffa|stroke-color2=#006400|stroke-width2=1|frame-latitude=34.732|frame-longitude=135.649}} {{location map|Japan Osaka Prefecture|float=center|width=205|label=Shijonawate Shrine|position=left|caption=}} }} '''Shijonawate Shrine''' (四條畷神社, Shijōnawate Jinja) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Shijonawate]], [[Osaka Prefecture]]. The shrine was formerly ranked as a [[Bekkaku Kanpeisha]] and is currently listed as a [[Beppyo Shrine]] under the jurisdiction of the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]]. It is one of the [[Kenmu Chuko Fifteen Shrines]] and is selected as one of the [[100 Famous Green Sites of Osaka]]. The shrine enshrines [[Masatsura Kusunoki]], who fought as a commander for the [[Southern Court (Japan)|Southern Court]] and died in the [[Battle of Shijonawate]]. While his father, [[Masashige Kusunoki]], is known as the "Great Kusunoki" (大楠公, Dai Nankō), his eldest son, Masatsura, is known as the "Small Kusunoki" (小楠公, Shō Nankō). Locally, the term "Kusunoki-san" often refers to Masatsura or the shrine itself. In [[1975]] (Showa 50), a town named "Kusunoki" was established, and several other places in the vicinity are named after him. The shrine grounds and surrounding areas are known for their cherry blossoms, attracting many visitors during the spring. == deity == * Main deity - [[Masatsura Kusunoki]] Additionally, 24 other warriors of the Kusunoki clan are enshrined: * [[Masatoki Kusunoki]], [[Masaie Kusunoki]], sons of Masaie Kusunoki * [[Yoshihide Wada]], [[Masatomo Wada]], Wada Kirokuzaemon, sons of Wada Kirokuzaemon * [[Iku Hisahide]], Hatakeyama Yosanosuke Toshiaki, Hatakeyama Rokurō, [[Noda Castle (Kawachi Province)|Noda Shiro]], sons of Noda Shiro * Kane no Shō (某), younger brother of Kane no Shō, Seki Jū Ryōen, son of Seki Jū Ryōen, [[Nishi A (name)|Miwa Nishi A]], son of Miwa Nishi A * Kawabe Ishikimaru, Hōda (某), Amaryōgan, Aoya Gyōbu == History == In [[1348]] (Shōhei 3), the [[Battle of Shijonawate]] took place here, resulting in the defeat of the Southern Court's forces by the Northern Court's army led by the brothers [[Tadayoshi Ashikaga|Tadayoshi]] and [[Takauji Ashikaga|Takauji]]. Masatsura Kusunoki and his brother [[Masatoki Kusunoki|Masatoki]] committed suicide, along with many others from the Kusunoki clan. Afterward, a tomb called "Kusunotsuka" was built for Masatsura in Nanino Village, and his spirit was quietly enshrined. In the [[Meiji era]], the government recognized the Southern Court as the legitimate ruling entity, leading to the deification of Masatsura's father, [[Masashige Kusunoki]], as the "Great Kusunoki" (大楠公). Masatsura, who followed his father's will and fought for the Southern Court, was also revered as the "Small Kusunoki" (小楠公). In [[1878]] (Meiji 11), his tomb was renamed "Small Kusunoki Imperial Mausoleum" and expanded. Around the same time, officials from Sumiyoshi Hirata Shrine near Mount Iimori petitioned the government to establish a shrine for the Kusunoki clan. The petition was granted, and on December 16, [[1889]] (Meiji 22), the founding of Shijonawate Shrine and its designation as a [[Bekkaku Kanpeisha]] were decreed[{{NDLDC|2945191}} Meiji 22 December 17 Official Gazette (No. 1942)] Ministry of the Interior Notice No. 61. The shrine was established in [[1890]] (Meiji 23) next to Sumiyoshi Hirata Shrine. The area around the shrine flourished, particularly after the Naniwa Railway opened a line from central [[Osaka]] to nearby Shijonawate Station in [[1895]] (Meiji 28). The name "Shijonawate" became associated with the area, and in [[1932]] (Showa 7), the village of Koka was renamed Shijonawate Village, later becoming Shijonawate City. The surrounding region developed into one of the largest urban areas in northern [[Kawachi Province]], a status it maintains today. In [[1948]] (Showa 23), the shrine was included in the list of [[Beppyo Shrines]] by the Association of Shinto Shrines. To the west of the shrine lies the Small Kusunoki Imperial Mausoleum (an Osaka Prefecture designated historic site), and to the northwest is the tomb of [[Yoshihide Wada]] (also a designated historic site). == Shrine Grounds == * main hall * Worship Hall * Statue of Masashige and Masatsura Kusunoki, depicting their farewell at Sakurai - Inscribed with "Loyalty and Filial Piety." * Miboyashi Shrine - Enshrines Kusunoki's mother, Kusako Taji no Mikoto. Sub-shrine. * Worship Place * Statue of Masatsura and his mother Kusako - Inscribed with "Loyalty and Filial Piety." * Torii - Gifted from Ise Grand Shrine in [[1990]] (Heisei 2) to commemorate the shrine's 100th anniversary. * Kusunoki Tenjin Shrine - Enshrines [[Sugawara no Michizane]] * Yugen Reikonsha - Enshrines the spirits of ancient and modern scholars and leaders. Originally enshrined at the Adult Education Training Center on the eastern side of Mount Iimori, it was relocated to the current site on April 7, [[2007]] (Heisei 19). * Kusunoki Memorial Tower - A memorial tower for Masashige Kusunoki. It has an unusual design with two round stones representing the water element, discovered in a mountain in Toyonaka City and moved to its current location in [[1991]] (Heisei 3). * Shrine Office * Rest Area - Once an observation deck, it now offers limited views due to tree growth. * Shijonawate Shrine Hall * Off-site ** Small Kusunoki Imperial Mausoleum (Osaka Prefecture designated historic site) - The tomb of Masatsura Kusunoki, featuring a designated camphor tree. ** Tomb of Yoshihide Wada (Osaka Prefecture designated historic site) == Cultural Properties == === Important Cultural Property === * Sword signed "Kanei" - [[Kamakura period]], deposited in the Osaka History Museum. === Osaka Prefecture Designated Historic Sites === * Tomb of Masatsura Kusunoki * Tomb of Yoshihide Wada === Osaka Prefecture Designated Natural Monument === * Camphor tree at the tomb of Masatsura Kusunoki == festivals == * Spring Grand festival on April 5 * Autumn Grand festival on October 5 == Pilgrimage Sites == ; [[Kenmu Chuko Fifteen Shrines]] ; [[Naniwa Seven Fortunes Pilgrimage]] ; [[Sacred Sites of the Kinki Region Pilgrimage]] : 60 [[Hiraoka Shrine]] - '''61 Shijonawate Shrine''' - 62 [[Minase Shrine]] == Transportation == * 15-minute walk from [[Shijonawate Station]] on the [[Katamachi Line]] (Gakkentoshi Line) of [[West Japan Railway Company]] == References == {{Reflist}} == Related Books == *[https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/1112175/1/1 Yamada Yonekichi, "History of the Fifteen Shrines of Imperial Restoration," compiled by Nirokushinsho, 1935, pp. 82-85. (National Diet Library Digital Collections)] * Anzu Motohiko and Umeda Yoshihiko (editors and supervisors), "Shinto Dictionary," [[Shinto Newspaper Company]], 1968, p. 32. * [[Eiji Shirai]] and Toki Masanori (editors), "Shrine Dictionary," [[Tokyo-do Publishing]], 1979, pp. 163-164. == Related Articles == * [[Small Kusunoki Imperial Mausoleum]] - Karasu Minami-cho. * Tomb of [[Yoshihide Wada]] - Minamino 4-chome. * [[Sakurai Station Ruins]] * [[Battle of Shijonawate]] * [[Nanko Katsura]] - A rakugo storyteller living in Shijonawate City who was influenced by the Kusunoki story when he took Originally enshrined at the Adult Education Training Center on the eastern side of Mount Iimori, it was relocated to the current site on April 7, [[2007]] (Heisei 19). * Kusunoki Memorial Tower - A memorial tower for Masashige Kusunoki. It has an unusual design with two round stones representing the water element, discovered in a mountain in Toyonaka City and moved to its current location in [[1991]] (Heisei 3). * Shrine Office * Rest Area - Once an observation deck, it now offers limited views due to tree growth. * Shijonawate Shrine Hall * Off-site ** Small Kusunoki Imperial Mausoleum (Osaka Prefecture designated historic site) - The tomb of Masatsura Kusunoki, featuring a designated camphor tree. ** Tomb of Yoshihide Wada (Osaka Prefecture designated historic site) == Cultural Properties == === Important Cultural Property === * Sword signed "Kanei" - [[Kamakura period]], deposited in the Osaka History Museum. === Osaka Prefecture Designated Historic Sites === * Tomb of Masatsura Kusunoki * Tomb of Yoshihide Wada === Osaka Prefecture Designated Natural Monument === * Camphor tree at the tomb of Masatsura Kusunoki == festivals == * Spring Grand festival on April 5 * Autumn Grand festival on October 5 == Pilgrimage Sites == ; [[Kenmu Chuko Fifteen Shrines]] ; [[Naniwa Seven Fortunes Pilgrimage]] ; [[Sacred Sites of the Kinki Region Pilgrimage]] : 60 [[Hiraoka Shrine]] - '''61 Shijonawate Shrine''' - 62 [[Minase Shrine]] == Transportation == * 15-minute walk from [[Shijonawate Station]] on the [[Katamachi Line]] (Gakkentoshi Line) of [[West Japan Railway Company]] == References == {{Reflist}} == Related Books == *[https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/1112175/1/1 Yamada Yonekichi, "History of the Fifteen Shrines of Imperial Restoration," compiled by Nirokushinsho, 1935, pp. 82-85. {{Short description|Shinto shrine located in Shijōnawate, Osaka Prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = {{nihongo|'''Shijōnawate Shrine'''|四條畷神社}} | image = Shijonawate Jinja Honden 2018.jpg | caption = Shijōnawate Jinja [[Honden]] | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34.73245|135.64917|type:landmark_region:JP|format=dms|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} '''Shijōnawate Shrine''' (四條畷神社, ''Shijōnawate jinja'') is a [[Shinto]] shrine located in [[Shijōnawate]], [[Osaka Prefecture]], Japan. Its main festival is held annually on February 12. It was founded in 1890, and enshrines [[Kusunoki Masatsura]] along with 24 other [[kami]]. It is one of the [[Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]]. ==Overview== {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Shijonawate Shrine | image = [[image:Shijonawate Jinja Honden 2018.jpg|250px]]
Worship Hall | location = Minamino 2-18-1, [[Shijonawate]], [[Osaka Prefecture]] | coordinates = {{coord|34|43|56.7|N|135|38|57|E|region:JP-27|display=it}} | deity = [[Masatsura Kusunoki]] and 24 others | Shrine rank = [[Bekkaku Kanpeisha]]
[[Beppyo Shrine]] | established = [[1890]] (Meiji 23) | main hall = | alternate names = | Pilgrimage = [[Kenmu Chuko Fifteen Shrines]]
[[Naniwa Seven Fortunes Pilgrimage]]
[[Sacred Sites of the Kinki Region Pilgrimage]] No. 61 (Osaka No. 20) | festival = Spring Grand festival on April 5, Autumn Grand festival on October 5 | ceremony = | map = {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=205|frame-height=200|marker=religious-shinto|type2=shape|fill2=#f5fffa|stroke-color2=#006400|stroke-width2=1|frame-latitude=34.732|frame-longitude=135.649}} {{location map|Japan Osaka Prefecture|float=center|width=205|label=Shijonawate Shrine|position=left|caption=}} }} '''Shijonawate Shrine''' (四條畷神社, Shijōnawate Jinja) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Shijonawate]], [[Osaka Prefecture]]. The shrine was formerly ranked as a [[Bekkaku Kanpeisha]] and is currently listed as a [[Beppyo Shrine]] under the jurisdiction of the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]]. It is one of the [[Kenmu Chuko Fifteen Shrines]] and is selected as one of the [[100 Famous Green Sites of Osaka]]. The shrine enshrines [[Masatsura Kusunoki]], who fought as a commander for the [[Southern Court (Japan)|Southern Court]] and died in the [[Battle of Shijonawate]]. While his father, [[Masashige Kusunoki]], is known as the "Great Kusunoki" (大楠公, Dai Nankō), his eldest son, Masatsura, is known as the "Small Kusunoki" (小楠公, Shō Nankō). Locally, the term "Kusunoki-san" often refers to Masatsura or the shrine itself. In [[1975]] (Showa 50), a town named "Kusunoki" was established, and several other places in the vicinity are named after him. The shrine grounds and surrounding areas are known for their cherry blossoms, attracting many visitors during the spring. == deity == * Main deity - [[Masatsura Kusunoki]] Additionally, 24 other warriors of the Kusunoki clan are enshrined: * [[Masatoki Kusunoki]], [[Masaie Kusunoki]], sons of Masaie Kusunoki * [[Yoshihide Wada]], [[Masatomo Wada]], Wada Kirokuzaemon, sons of Wada Kirokuzaemon * [[Iku Hisahide]], Hatakeyama Yosanosuke Toshiaki, Hatakeyama Rokurō, [[Noda Castle (Kawachi Province)|Noda Shiro]], sons of Noda Shiro * Kane no Shō (某), younger brother of Kane no Shō, Seki Jū Ryōen, son of Seki Jū Ryōen, [[Nishi A (name)|Miwa Nishi A]], son of Miwa Nishi A * Kawabe Ishikimaru, Hōda (某), Amaryōgan, Aoya Gyōbu == History == In [[1348]] (Shōhei 3), the [[Battle of Shijonawate]] took place here, resulting in the defeat of the Southern Court's forces by the Northern Court's army led by the brothers [[Tadayoshi Ashikaga|Tadayoshi]] and [[Takauji Ashikaga|Takauji]]. Masatsura Kusunoki and his brother [[Masatoki Kusunoki|Masatoki]] committed suicide, along with many others from the Kusunoki clan. Afterward, a tomb called "Kusunotsuka" was built for Masatsura in Nanino Village, and his spirit was quietly enshrined. In the [[Meiji era]], the government recognized the Southern Court as the legitimate ruling entity, leading to the deification of Masatsura's father, [[Masashige Kusunoki]], as the "Great Kusunoki" (大楠公). Masatsura, who followed his father's will and fought for the Southern Court, was also revered as the "Small Kusunoki" (小楠公). In [[1878]] (Meiji 11), his tomb was renamed "Small Kusunoki Imperial Mausoleum" and expanded. Around the same time, officials from Sumiyoshi Hirata Shrine near Mount Iimori petitioned the government to establish a shrine for the Kusunoki clan. The petition was granted, and on December 16, [[1889]] (Meiji 22), the founding of Shijonawate Shrine and its designation as a [[Bekkaku Kanpeisha]] were decreed[{{NDLDC|2945191}} Meiji 22 December 17 Official Gazette (No. 1942)] Ministry of the Interior Notice No. 61. The shrine was established in [[1890]] (Meiji 23) next to Sumiyoshi Hirata Shrine. The area around the shrine flourished, particularly after the Naniwa Railway opened a line from central [[Osaka]] to nearby Shijonawate Station in [[1895]] (Meiji 28). The name "Shijonawate" became associated with the area, and in [[1932]] (Showa 7), the village of Koka was renamed Shijonawate Village, later becoming Shijonawate City. The surrounding region developed into one of the largest urban areas in northern [[Kawachi Province]], a status it maintains today. In [[1948]] (Showa 23), the shrine was included in the list of [[Beppyo Shrines]] by the Association of Shinto Shrines. To the west of the shrine lies the Small Kusunoki Imperial Mausoleum (an Osaka Prefecture designated historic site), and to the northwest is the tomb of [[Yoshihide Wada]] (also a designated historic site). == Shrine Grounds == * main hall * Worship Hall * Statue of Masashige and Masatsura Kusunoki, depicting their farewell at Sakurai - Inscribed with "Loyalty and Filial Piety." * Miboyashi Shrine - Enshrines Kusunoki's mother, Kusako Taji no Mikoto. Sub-shrine. * Worship Place * Statue of Masatsura and his mother Kusako - Inscribed with "Loyalty and Filial Piety." * Torii - Gifted from Ise Grand Shrine in [[1990]] (Heisei 2) to commemorate the shrine's 100th anniversary. * Kusunoki Tenjin Shrine - Enshrines [[Sugawara no Michizane]] * Yugen Reikonsha - Enshrines the spirits of ancient and modern scholars and leaders. Originally enshrined at the Adult Education Training Center on the eastern side of Mount Iimori, it was relocated to the current site on April 7, [[2007]] (Heisei 19). * Kusunoki Memorial Tower - A memorial tower for Masashige Kusunoki. It has an unusual design with two round stones representing the water element, discovered in a mountain in Toyonaka City and moved to its current location in [[1991]] (Heisei 3). * Shrine Office * Rest Area - Once an observation deck, it now offers limited views due to tree growth. * Shijonawate Shrine Hall * Off-site ** Small Kusunoki Imperial Mausoleum (Osaka Prefecture designated historic site) - The tomb of Masatsura Kusunoki, featuring a designated camphor tree. ** Tomb of Yoshihide Wada (Osaka Prefecture designated historic site) == Cultural Properties == === Important Cultural Property === * Sword signed "Kanei" - [[Kamakura period]], deposited in the Osaka History Museum. === Osaka Prefecture Designated Historic Sites === * Tomb of Masatsura Kusunoki * Tomb of Yoshihide Wada === Osaka Prefecture Designated Natural Monument === * Camphor tree at the tomb of Masatsura Kusunoki == festivals == * Spring Grand festival on April 5 * Autumn Grand festival on October 5 == Pilgrimage Sites == ; [[Kenmu Chuko Fifteen Shrines]] ; [[Naniwa Seven Fortunes Pilgrimage]] ; [[Sacred Sites of the Kinki Region Pilgrimage]] : 60 [[Hiraoka Shrine]] - '''61 Shijonawate Shrine''' - 62 [[Minase Shrine]] == Transportation == * 15-minute walk from [[Shijonawate Station]] on the [[Katamachi Line]] (Gakkentoshi Line) of [[West Japan Railway Company]] == References == {{Reflist}} == Related Books == *[https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/1112175/1/1 Yamada Yonekichi, "History of the Fifteen Shrines of Imperial Restoration," compiled by Nirokushinsho, 1935, pp. 82-85. (National Diet Library Digital Collections)] * Anzu Motohiko and Umeda Yoshihiko (editors and supervisors), "Shinto Dictionary," [[Shinto Newspaper Company]], 1968, p. 32. * [[Eiji Shirai]] and Toki Masanori (editors), "Shrine Dictionary," [[Tokyo-do Publishing]], 1979, pp. 163-164. == Related Articles == * [[Small Kusunoki Imperial Mausoleum]] - Karasu Minami-cho. * Tomb of [[Yoshihide Wada]] - Minamino 4-chome. * [[Sakurai Station Ruins]] * [[Battle of Shijonawate]] * [[Nanko Katsura]] - A rakugo storyteller living in Shijonawate City who was influenced by the Kusunoki story when he took {{Short description|Shinto shrine located in Shijōnawate, Osaka Prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = {{nihongo|'''Shijōnawate Shrine'''|四條畷神社}} | image = Shijonawate Jinja Honden 2018.jpg | caption = Shijōnawate Jinja [[Honden]] | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34.73245|135.64917|type:landmark_region:JP|format=dms|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} '''Shijōnawate Shrine''' (四條畷神社, ''Shijōnawate jinja'') is a [[Shinto]] shrine located in [[Shijōnawate]], [[Osaka Prefecture]], Japan. Its main festival is held annually on February 12. It was founded in 1890, and enshrines [[Kusunoki Masatsura]] along with 24 other [[kami]]. It is one of the [[Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]]. ==Overview== {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Shijonawate Shrine | image = [[image:Shijonawate Jinja Honden 2018.jpg|250px]]
Worship Hall | location = Minamino 2-18-1, [[Shijonawate]], [[Osaka Prefecture]] | coordinates = {{coord|34|43|56.7|N|135|38|57|E|region:JP-27|display=it}} | deity = [[Masatsura Kusunoki]] and 24 others | Shrine rank = [[Bekkaku Kanpeisha]]
[[Beppyo Shrine]] | established = [[1890]] (Meiji 23) | main hall = | alternate names = | Pilgrimage = [[Kenmu Chuko Fifteen Shrines]]
[[Naniwa Seven Fortunes Pilgrimage]]
[[Sacred Sites of the Kinki Region Pilgrimage]] No. 61 (Osaka No. 20) | festival = Spring Grand festival on April 5, Autumn Grand festival on October 5 | ceremony = | map = {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=205|frame-height=200|marker=religious-shinto|type2=shape|fill2=#f5fffa|stroke-color2=#006400|stroke-width2=1|frame-latitude=34.732|frame-longitude=135.649}} {{location map|Japan Osaka Prefecture|float=center|width=205|label=Shijonawate Shrine|position=left|caption=}} }} '''Shijonawate Shrine''' (四條畷神社, Shijōnawate Jinja) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Shijonawate]], [[Osaka Prefecture]]. The shrine was formerly ranked as a [[Bekkaku Kanpeisha]] and is currently listed as a [[Beppyo Shrine]] under the jurisdiction of the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]]. It is one of the [[Kenmu Chuko Fifteen Shrines]] and is selected as one of the [[100 Famous Green Sites of Osaka]]. The shrine enshrines [[Masatsura Kusunoki]], who fought as a commander for the [[Southern Court (Japan)|Southern Court]] and died in the [[Battle of Shijonawate]]. While his father, [[Masashige Kusunoki]], is known as the "Great Kusunoki" (大楠公, Dai Nankō), his eldest son, Masatsura, is known as the "Small Kusunoki" (小楠公, Shō Nankō). Locally, the term "Kusunoki-san" often refers to Masatsura or the shrine itself. In [[1975]] (Showa 50), a town named "Kusunoki" was established, and several other places in the vicinity are named after him. The shrine grounds and surrounding areas are known for their cherry blossoms, attracting many visitors during the spring. == deity == * Main deity - [[Masatsura Kusunoki]] Additionally, 24 other warriors of the Kusunoki clan are enshrined: * [[Masatoki Kusunoki]], [[Masaie Kusunoki]], sons of Masaie Kusunoki * [[Yoshihide Wada]], [[Masatomo Wada]], Wada Kirokuzaemon, sons of Wada Kirokuzaemon * [[Iku Hisahide]], Hatakeyama Yosanosuke Toshiaki, Hatakeyama Rokurō, [[Noda Castle (Kawachi Province)|Noda Shiro]], sons of Noda Shiro * Kane no Shō (某), younger brother of Kane no Shō, Seki Jū Ryōen, son of Seki Jū Ryōen, [[Nishi A (name)|Miwa Nishi A]], son of Miwa Nishi A * Kawabe Ishikimaru, Hōda (某), Amaryōgan, Aoya Gyōbu == History == In [[1348]] (Shōhei 3), the [[Battle of Shijonawate]] took place here, resulting in the defeat of the Southern Court's forces by the Northern Court's army led by the brothers [[Tadayoshi Ashikaga|Tadayoshi]] and [[Takauji Ashikaga|Takauji]]. Masatsura Kusunoki and his brother [[Masatoki Kusunoki|Masatoki]] committed suicide, along with many others from the Kusunoki clan. Afterward, a tomb called "Kusunotsuka" was built for Masatsura in Nanino Village, and his spirit was quietly enshrined. In the [[Meiji era]], the government recognized the Southern Court as the legitimate ruling entity, leading to the deification of Masatsura's father, [[Masashige Kusunoki]], as the "Great Kusunoki" (大楠公). Masatsura, who followed his father's will and fought for the Southern Court, was also revered as the "Small Kusunoki" (小楠公). In [[1878]] (Meiji 11), his tomb was renamed "Small Kusunoki Imperial Mausoleum" and expanded. Around the same time, officials from Sumiyoshi Hirata Shrine near Mount Iimori petitioned the government to establish a shrine for the Kusunoki clan. The petition was granted, and on December 16, [[1889]] (Meiji 22), the founding of Shijonawate Shrine and its designation as a [[Bekkaku Kanpeisha]] were decreed[{{NDLDC|2945191}} Meiji 22 December 17 Official Gazette (No. 1942)] Ministry of the Interior Notice No. 61. The shrine was established in [[1890]] (Meiji 23) next to Sumiyoshi Hirata Shrine. The area around the shrine flourished, particularly after the Naniwa Railway opened a line from central [[Osaka]] to nearby Shijonawate Station in [[1895]] (Meiji 28). The name "Shijonawate" became associated with the area, and in [[1932]] (Showa 7), the village of Koka was renamed Shijonawate Village, later becoming Shijonawate City. The surrounding region developed into one of the largest urban areas in northern [[Kawachi Province]], a status it maintains today. In [[1948]] (Showa 23), the shrine was included in the list of [[Beppyo Shrines]] by the Association of Shinto Shrines. To the west of the shrine lies the Small Kusunoki Imperial Mausoleum (an Osaka Prefecture designated historic site), and to the northwest is the tomb of [[Yoshihide Wada]] (also a designated historic site). == Shrine Grounds == * main hall * Worship Hall * Statue of Masashige and Masatsura Kusunoki, depicting their farewell at Sakurai - Inscribed with "Loyalty and Filial Piety." * Miboyashi Shrine - Enshrines Kusunoki's mother, Kusako Taji no Mikoto. Sub-shrine. * Worship Place * Statue of Masatsura and his mother Kusako - Inscribed with "Loyalty and Filial Piety." * Torii - Gifted from Ise Grand Shrine in [[1990]] (Heisei 2) to commemorate the shrine's 100th anniversary. * Kusunoki Tenjin Shrine - Enshrines [[Sugawara no Michizane]] * Yugen Reikonsha - Enshrines the spirits of ancient and modern scholars and leaders. Originally enshrined at the Adult Education Training Center on the eastern side of Mount Iimori, it was relocated to the current site on April 7, [[2007]] (Heisei 19). * Kusunoki Memorial Tower - A memorial tower for Masashige Kusunoki. It has an unusual design with two round stones representing the water element, discovered in a mountain in Toyonaka City and moved to its current location in [[1991]] (Heisei 3). * Shrine Office * Rest Area - Once an observation deck, it now offers limited views due to tree growth. * Shijonawate Shrine Hall * Off-site ** Small Kusunoki Imperial Mausoleum (Osaka Prefecture designated historic site) - The tomb of Masatsura Kusunoki, featuring a designated camphor tree. ** Tomb of Yoshihide Wada (Osaka Prefecture designated historic site) == Cultural Properties == === Important Cultural Property === * Sword signed "Kanei" - [[Kamakura period]], deposited in the Osaka History Museum. === Osaka Prefecture Designated Historic Sites === * Tomb of Masatsura Kusunoki * Tomb of Yoshihide Wada === Osaka Prefecture Designated Natural Monument === * Camphor tree at the tomb of Masatsura Kusunoki == festivals == * Spring Grand festival on April 5 * Autumn Grand festival on October 5 == Pilgrimage Sites == ; [[Kenmu Chuko Fifteen Shrines]] ; [[Naniwa Seven Fortunes Pilgrimage]] ; [[Sacred Sites of the Kinki Region Pilgrimage]] : 60 [[Hiraoka Shrine]] - '''61 Shijonawate Shrine''' - 62 [[Minase Shrine]] == Transportation == * 15-minute walk from [[Shijonawate Station]] on the [[Katamachi Line]] (Gakkentoshi Line) of [[West Japan Railway Company]] == References == {{Reflist}} == Related Books == *[https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/1112175/1/1 Yamada Yonekichi, "History of the Fifteen Shrines of Imperial Restoration," compiled by Nirokushinsho, 1935, pp. 82-85. (National Diet Library Digital Collections)] * Anzu Motohiko and Umeda Yoshihiko (editors and supervisors), "Shinto Dictionary," [[Shinto Newspaper Company]], 1968, p. 32. * [[Eiji Shirai]] and Toki Masanori (editors), "Shrine Dictionary," [[Tokyo-do Publishing]], 1979, pp. 163-164. == Related Articles == * [[Small Kusunoki Imperial Mausoleum]] - Karasu Minami-cho. * Tomb of [[Yoshihide Wada]] - Minamino 4-chome. * [[Sakurai Station Ruins]] * [[Battle of Shijonawate]] * [[Nanko Katsura]] - A rakugo storyteller living in Shijonawate City who was influenced by the Kusunoki story when he took
