Its main festival is held annually on February 12. It was founded in 1890, and enshrines {{ill|Kusunoki Masatsura|en|Kusunoki Masatsura|WD=Q966298|lt=Kusunoki Masatsura}} along with 24 other [[kami]]. It is one of the [[List of the Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration|Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]]. ==Overview== {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Shijonawate Shrine | image =
Worship Hall | location = Minamino 2-18-1, [[:en:Shijonawate|Shijonawate]], {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|en|Osaka Prefecture|WD=Q122723|lt=Osaka Prefecture}} | coordinates = {{coord|34|43|56.7|N|135|38|57|E|region:JP-27|display=it}} | deity = [[:en:Masatsura Kusunoki|Masatsura Kusunoki]] and 24 others | Shrine Rank = [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Bekkaku Kanpeisha]]

[[Naniwa Seven Fortunes Pilgrimage]]
[[Sacred Sites of the Kinki Region Pilgrimage]] No. 61 (Osaka No. 20) | festival = Spring Grand festival on April 5, Autumn Grand festival on October 5 | ceremony = | map = {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=205|frame-height=200|marker=religious-shinto|type2=shape|fill2=#f5fffa|stroke-color2=#006400|stroke-width2=1|frame-latitude=34.732|frame-longitude=135.649}} {{location map|Japan Osaka Prefecture|float=center|width=205|label=Shijonawate Shrine|position=left|caption=}} }} '''Shijonawate Shrine''' (四條畷神社, Shijōnawate Jinja) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}} located in [[:en:Shijonawate|Shijonawate]], {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|en|Osaka Prefecture|WD=Q122723|lt=Osaka Prefecture}}. The shrine was formerly Ranked as a [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Bekkaku Kanpeisha]] and is currently listed as a [[Beppyo Shrine]] under the jurisdiction of the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]]. It is one of the [[Kenmu Chuko Fifteen Shrines]] and is selected as one of the [[100 Famous Green Sites of Osaka]]. The shrine enshrines [[:en:Masatsura Kusunoki|Masatsura Kusunoki]], who fought as a commander for the [[Southern Court (Japan)|Southern Court]] and died in the [[:en:Battle of Shijonawate|Battle of Shijonawate]]. While his father, [[:en:Masashige Kusunoki|Masashige Kusunoki]], is known as the "Great Kusunoki" (大楠公, Dai Nankō), his eldest son, Masatsura, is known as the "Small Kusunoki" (小楠公, Shō Nankō). Locally, the term "Kusunoki-san" often refers to Masatsura or the shrine itself. In {{ill|1975|en|1975|WD=Q2479|lt=1975}} (Showa 50), a town named "Kusunoki" was established, and several other places in the vicinity are named after him. The shrine grounds and surrounding areas are known for their cherry blossoms, attracting many visitors during the spring. == deity == * Main deity - [[:en:Masatsura Kusunoki|Masatsura Kusunoki]] Additionally, 24 other warriors of the Kusunoki clan are enshrined: * [[Masatoki Kusunoki]], [[:en:Masaie Kusunoki|Masaie Kusunoki]], sons of Masaie Kusunoki * [[Yoshihide Wada]], [[Masatomo Wada]], Wada Kirokuzaemon, sons of Wada Kirokuzaemon * [[Iku Hisahide]], Hatakeyama Yosanosuke Toshiaki, Hatakeyama Rokurō, [[Noda Castle (Kawachi Province)|Noda Shiro]], sons of Noda Shiro * Kane no Shō (某), younger brother of Kane no Shō, Seki Jū Ryōen, son of Seki Jū Ryōen, [[Nishi A (name)|Miwa Nishi A]], son of Miwa Nishi A * Kawabe Ishikimaru, Hōda (某), Amaryōgan, Aoya Gyōbu == History == In {{ill|1348|en|1348|WD=Q6085|lt=1348}} (Shōhei 3), the [[:en:Battle of Shijonawate|Battle of Shijonawate]] took place here, resulting in the defeat of the Southern Court's forces by the Northern Court's army led by the brothers [[Tadayoshi Ashikaga|Tadayoshi]] and [[:en:Takauji Ashikaga|Takauji]]. Masatsura Kusunoki and his brother [[Masatoki Kusunoki|Masatoki]] committed suicide, along with many others from the Kusunoki clan. Afterward, a tomb called "Kusunotsuka" was built for Masatsura in Nanino Village, and his spirit was quietly enshrined. In the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era}}, the government recognized the Southern Court as the legitimate ruling entity, leading to the deification of Masatsura's father, [[:en:Masashige Kusunoki|Masashige Kusunoki]], as the "Great Kusunoki" (大楠公). Masatsura, who followed his father's will and fought for the Southern Court, was also revered as the "Small Kusunoki" (小楠公). In {{ill|1878|en|1878|WD=Q7776|lt=1878}} (Meiji 11), his tomb was renamed "Small Kusunoki Imperial Mausoleum" and expanded. Around the same time, officials from Sumiyoshi Hirata Shrine near Mount Iimori petitioned the government to establish a shrine for the Kusunoki clan. The petition was granted, and on December 16, {{ill|1889|en|1889|WD=Q7831|lt=1889}} (Meiji 22), the founding of Shijonawate Shrine and its designation as a [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Bekkaku Kanpeisha]] were decreed[{{NDLDC|2945191}} Meiji 22 December 17 Official Gazette (No. 1942)] Ministry of the Interior Notice No. 61. The shrine was established in {{ill|1890|en|1890|WD=Q7832|lt=1890}} (Meiji 23) next to Sumiyoshi Hirata Shrine. The area around the shrine flourished, particularly after the Naniwa Railway opened a line from central {{ill|Osaka|en|Osaka|WD=Q35765|lt=Osaka}} to nearby Shijonawate Station in {{ill|1895|en|1895|WD=Q7844|lt=1895}} (Meiji 28). The name "Shijonawate" became associated with the area, and in {{ill|1932|en|1932|WD=Q18743|lt=1932}} (Showa 7), the village of Koka was renamed Shijonawate Village, later becoming Shijonawate City. The surrounding region developed into one of the largest urban areas in northern {{ill|Kawachi Province|en|Kawachi Province|WD=Q1047150|lt=Kawachi Province}}, a status it maintains today. In {{ill|1948|en|1948|WD=Q5165|lt=1948}} (Showa 23), the shrine was included in the list of [[Beppyo Shrine]] by the Association of Shinto Shrines. To the west of the shrine lies the Small Kusunoki Imperial Mausoleum (an Osaka Prefecture designated historic site), and to the northwest is the tomb of [[Yoshihide Wada]] (also a designated historic site). == Shrine Grounds == * main hall * Worship Hall * Statue of Masashige and Masatsura Kusunoki, depicting their farewell at Sakurai - Inscribed with "Loyalty and Filial Piety." * Miboyashi Shrine - Enshrines Kusunoki's mother, Kusako Taji no Mikoto. Sub-shrine. * Worship Place * Statue of Masatsura and his mother Kusako - Inscribed with "Loyalty and Filial Piety." * Torii - Gifted from Ise Grand Shrine in {{ill|1990|en|1990|WD=Q2064|lt=1990}} (Heisei 2) to commemorate the shrine's 100th anniversary. * Kusunoki Tenjin Shrine - Enshrines {{ill|Sugawara no Michizane|en|Sugawara no Michizane|WD=Q382005|lt=Sugawara no Michizane}} * Yugen Reikonsha - Enshrines the spirits of ancient and modern scholars and leaders. Originally enshrined at the Adult Education Training Center on the eastern side of Mount Iimori, it was relocated to the current site on April 7, {{ill|2007|en|2007|WD=Q2024|lt=2007}} (Heisei 19). * Kusunoki Memorial Tower - A memorial tower for Masashige Kusunoki. It has an unusual design with two round stones representing the water element, discovered in a mountain in Toyonaka City and moved to its current location in {{ill|1991|en|1991|WD=Q2062|lt=1991}} (Heisei 3). * Shrine Office * Rest Area - Once an observation deck, it now offers limited views due to tree growth. * Shijonawate Shrine Hall * Off-site ** Small Kusunoki Imperial Mausoleum (Osaka Prefecture designated historic site) - The tomb of Masatsura Kusunoki, featuring a designated camphor tree. ** Tomb of Yoshihide Wada (Osaka Prefecture designated historic site) == Cultural Properties == === Important Cultural Property === * Sword signed "Kanei" - {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|WD=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period}}, deposited in the Osaka History Museum. === Osaka Prefecture Designated Historic Sites === * Tomb of Masatsura Kusunoki * Tomb of Yoshihide Wada === Osaka Prefecture Designated Natural Monument === * Camphor tree at the tomb of Masatsura Kusunoki == festivals == * Spring Grand festival on April 5 * Autumn Grand festival on October 5 == Pilgrimage Sites == ; [[Kenmu Chuko Fifteen Shrines]] ; [[Naniwa Seven Fortunes Pilgrimage]] ; [[Sacred Sites of the Kinki Region Pilgrimage]] : 60 [[Hiraoka Shrine]] * '''61 Shijonawate Shrine''' - 62 [[Minase Jingu|Minase Shrine]] == Transportation == * 15-minute walk from [[:en:Shijonawate Station|Shijonawate Station]] on the {{ill|Katamachi Line|en|Katamachi Line|WD=Q1073379|lt=Katamachi Line}} (Gakkentoshi Line) of {{ill|West Japan Railway Company|en|West Japan Railway Company|WD=Q502125|lt=West Japan Railway Company}} == References == {{Reflist}} == Related Books == *[https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/1112175/1/1 Yamada Yonekichi, "History of the Fifteen Shrines of Imperial Restoration," compiled by Nirokushinsho, 1935, pp. 82-85. (National Diet Library Digital Collections)] * Anzu Motohiko and Umeda Yoshihiko (editors and supervisors), "Shinto Dictionary," [[Shinto Newspaper Company]], 1968, p. 32. * {{ill|Eiji Shirai|en|Eiji Shirai|WD=Q17216364|lt=Eiji Shirai}} and Toki Masanori (editors), "Shrine Dictionary," [[Tokyo-do Publishing]], 1979, pp. 163-164. == Related Articles == * [[Small Kusunoki Imperial Mausoleum]] - [[Sacred Sites of the Kinki Region Pilgrimage]] No. 61 (Osaka No. 20) | festival = Spring Grand festival on April 5, Autumn Grand festival on October 5 | ceremony = | map = {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=205|frame-height=200|marker=religious-shinto|type2=shape|fill2=#f5fffa|stroke-color2=#006400|stroke-width2=1|frame-latitude=34.732|frame-longitude=135.649}} {{location map|Japan Osaka Prefecture|float=center|width=205|label=Shijonawate Shrine|position=left|caption=}} }} '''Shijonawate Shrine''' (四條畷神社, Shijōnawate Jinja) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}} located in [[:en:Shijonawate|Shijonawate]], {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|en|Osaka Prefecture|WD=Q122723|lt=Osaka Prefecture}}. The shrine was formerly Ranked as a [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Bekkaku Kanpeisha]] and is currently listed as a [[Beppyo Shrine]] under the jurisdiction of the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]]. It is one of the [[Kenmu Chuko Fifteen Shrines]] and is selected as one of the [[100 Famous Green Sites of Osaka]]. The shrine enshrines [[:en:Masatsura Kusunoki|Masatsura Kusunoki]], who fought as a commander for the [[Southern Court (Japan)|Southern Court]] and died in the [[:en:Battle of Shijonawate|Battle of Shijonawate]]. While his father, [[:en:Masashige Kusunoki|Masashige Kusunoki]], is known as the "Great Kusunoki" (大楠公, Dai Nankō), his eldest son, Masatsura, is known as the "Small Kusunoki" (小楠公, Shō Nankō). Locally, the term "Kusunoki-san" often refers to Masatsura or the shrine itself. In {{ill|1975|en|1975|WD=Q2479|lt=1975}} (Showa 50), a town named "Kusunoki" was established, and several other places in the vicinity are named after him. The shrine grounds and surrounding areas are known for their cherry blossoms, attracting many visitors during the spring. == deity == * Main deity - [[:en:Masatsura Kusunoki|Masatsura Kusunoki]] Additionally, 24 other warriors of the Kusunoki clan are enshrined: * [[Masatoki Kusunoki]], [[:en:Masaie Kusunoki|Masaie Kusunoki]], sons of Masaie Kusunoki * [[Yoshihide Wada]], [[Masatomo Wada]], Wada Kirokuzaemon, sons of Wada Kirokuzaemon * [[Iku Hisahide]], Hatakeyama Yosanosuke Toshiaki, Hatakeyama Rokurō, [[Noda Castle (Kawachi Province)|Noda Shiro]], sons of Noda Shiro * Kane no Shō (某), younger brother of Kane no Shō, Seki Jū Ryōen, son of Seki Jū Ryōen, [[Nishi A (name)|Miwa Nishi A]], son of Miwa Nishi A * Kawabe Ishikimaru, Hōda (某), Amaryōgan, Aoya Gyōbu == History == In {{ill|1348|en|1348|WD=Q6085|lt=1348}} (Shōhei 3), the [[:en:Battle of Shijonawate|Battle of Shijonawate]] took place here, resulting in the defeat of the Southern Court's forces by the Northern Court's army led by the brothers [[Tadayoshi Ashikaga|Tadayoshi]] and [[:en:Takauji Ashikaga|Takauji]]. Masatsura Kusunoki and his brother [[Masatoki Kusunoki|Masatoki]] committed suicide, along with many others from the Kusunoki clan. Afterward, a tomb called "Kusunotsuka" was built for Masatsura in Nanino Village, and his spirit was quietly enshrined. In the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era}}, the government recognized the Southern Court as the legitimate ruling entity, leading to the deification of Masatsura's father, [[:en:Masashige Kusunoki|Masashige Kusunoki]], as the "Great Kusunoki" (大楠公). Masatsura, who followed his father's will and fought for the Southern Court, was also revered as the "Small Kusunoki" (小楠公). In {{ill|1878|en|1878|WD=Q7776|lt=1878}} (Meiji 11), his tomb was renamed "Small Kusunoki Imperial Mausoleum" and expanded. Around the same time, officials from Sumiyoshi Hirata Shrine near Mount Iimori petitioned the government to establish a shrine for the Kusunoki clan. The petition was granted, and on December 16, {{ill|1889|en|1889|WD=Q7831|lt=1889}} (Meiji 22), the founding of Shijonawate Shrine and its designation as a [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Bekkaku Kanpeisha]] were decreed[{{NDLDC|2945191}} Meiji 22 December 17 Official Gazette (No. 1942)] Ministry of the Interior Notice No. 61. The shrine was established in {{ill|1890|en|1890|WD=Q7832|lt=1890}} (Meiji 23) next to Sumiyoshi Hirata Shrine. The area around the shrine flourished, particularly after the Naniwa Railway opened a line from central {{ill|Osaka|en|Osaka|WD=Q35765|lt=Osaka}} to nearby Shijonawate Station in {{ill|1895|en|1895|WD=Q7844|lt=1895}} (Meiji 28). The name "Shijonawate" became associated with the area, and in {{ill|1932|en|1932|WD=Q18743|lt=1932}} (Showa 7), the village of Koka was renamed Shijonawate Village, later becoming Shijonawate City. The surrounding region developed into one of the largest urban areas in northern {{ill|Kawachi Province|en|Kawachi Province|WD=Q1047150|lt=Kawachi Province}}, a status it maintains today. In {{ill|1948|en|1948|WD=Q5165|lt=1948}} (Showa 23), the shrine was included in the list of [[Beppyo Shrine]] by the Association of Shinto Shrines. To the west of the shrine lies the Small Kusunoki Imperial Mausoleum (an Osaka Prefecture designated historic site), and to the northwest is the tomb of [[Yoshihide Wada]] (also a designated historic site). == Shrine Grounds == * main hall * Worship Hall * Statue of Masashige and Masatsura Kusunoki, depicting their farewell at Sakurai - Inscribed with "Loyalty and Filial Piety." * Miboyashi Shrine - Enshrines Kusunoki's mother, Kusako Taji no Mikoto. Sub-shrine. * Worship Place * Statue of Masatsura and his mother Kusako - Inscribed with "Loyalty and Filial Piety." * Torii - Gifted from Ise Grand Shrine in {{ill|1990|en|1990|WD=Q2064|lt=1990}} (Heisei 2) to commemorate the shrine's 100th anniversary. * Kusunoki Tenjin Shrine - Enshrines {{ill|Sugawara no Michizane|en|Sugawara no Michizane|WD=Q382005|lt=Sugawara no Michizane}} * Yugen Reikonsha - Enshrines the spirits of ancient and modern scholars and leaders. Originally enshrined at the Adult Education Training Center on the eastern side of Mount Iimori, it was relocated to the current site on April 7, {{ill|2007|en|2007|WD=Q2024|lt=2007}} (Heisei 19). * Kusunoki Memorial Tower - A memorial tower for Masashige Kusunoki. It has an unusual design with two round stones representing the water element, discovered in a mountain in Toyonaka City and moved to its current location in {{ill|1991|en|1991|WD=Q2062|lt=1991}} (Heisei 3). * Shrine Office * Rest Area - Once an observation deck, it now offers limited views due to tree growth. * Shijonawate Shrine Hall * Off-site ** Small Kusunoki Imperial Mausoleum (Osaka Prefecture designated historic site) - The tomb of Masatsura Kusunoki, featuring a designated camphor tree. ** Tomb of Yoshihide Wada (Osaka Prefecture designated historic site) == Cultural Properties == === Important Cultural Property === * Sword signed "Kanei" - {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|WD=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period}}, deposited in the Osaka History Museum. === Osaka Prefecture Designated Historic Sites === * Tomb of Masatsura Kusunoki * Tomb of Yoshihide Wada === Osaka Prefecture Designated Natural Monument === * Camphor tree at the tomb of Masatsura Kusunoki == festivals == * Spring Grand festival on April 5 * Autumn Grand festival on October 5 == Pilgrimage Sites == ; [[Kenmu Chuko Fifteen Shrines]] ; [[Naniwa Seven Fortunes Pilgrimage]] ; [[Sacred Sites of the Kinki Region Pilgrimage]] : 60 [[Hiraoka Shrine]] * '''61 Shijonawate Shrine''' - 62 [[Minase Jingu|Minase Shrine]] == Transportation == * 15-minute walk from [[:en:Shijonawate Station|Shijonawate Station]] on the {{ill|Katamachi Line|en|Katamachi Line|WD=Q1073379|lt=Katamachi Line}} (Gakkentoshi Line) of {{ill|West Japan Railway Company|en|West Japan Railway Company|WD=Q502125|lt=West Japan Railway Company}} == References == {{Reflist}} == Related Books == *[https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/1112175/1/1 Yamada Yonekichi, "History of the Fifteen Shrines of Imperial Restoration," compiled by Nirokushinsho, 1935, pp. 82-85. (National Diet Library Digital Collections)] * Anzu Motohiko and Umeda Yoshihiko (editors and supervisors), "Shinto Dictionary," [[Shinto Newspaper Company]], 1968, p. 32. * {{ill|Eiji Shirai|en|Eiji Shirai|WD=Q17216364|lt=Eiji Shirai}} and Toki Masanori (editors), "Shrine Dictionary," [[Tokyo-do Publishing]], 1979, pp. 163-164. == Related Articles == * [[Small Kusunoki Imperial Mausoleum]] - Karasu Minami-cho. * Tomb of [[Yoshihide Wada]] - Minamino 4-chome. * [[Sakurai Station Ruins]] * [[:en:Battle of Shijonawate|Battle of Shijonawate]] * [[Nanko Katsura]] - A rakugo storyteller living in Shijonawate City who was influenced by the Kusunoki story when he took his name. {{Commonscat|Shijonawate Jinja}} {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]s in Osaka}} {{Osaka Green 100 Selection}} {{Shinto-stub}} ==See also== *[[List of the Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration|Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]] ==External links== *[http://ikutamajinja.jp Official website] *[http://www.japantimes.co.jp/news/2014/06/24/national/osakas-ikukunitama-shrine-set-revive-ritual-first-time-70-years/ Osaka's Ikukunitama Shrine set to revive ritual for first time in 70 years] {{Shinto-stub}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} {{Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration}} \ {{translated page|ja|四條畷神社|version=103868821|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[image:Shijonawate Jinja Honden 2018.jpg|250px]] [[en:Shijōnawate Shrine]] [[ja:四條畷神社]] [[en:Shijōnawate Shrine]] [[fr:Shijōnawate-jinja]] [[zh:四條畷神社]] [[fr:Shijōnawate-jinja]] [[zh:四條畷神社]] {{wikidata link|Q10925104}} [[Category:1890 establishments in Japan]] [[Category:Bekkaku Kanpeisha]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Buildings in Shijonawate, Osaka]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]] [[Category:Kusunoki clan]] [[Category:Kusunoki clan|Shrines]] [[Category:Shijonawate Jinja]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:translated pages with valid en interwikis]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:pages]]

Its main festival is held annually on February 12. It was founded in 1890, and enshrines {{ill|Kusunoki Masatsura|en|Kusunoki Masatsura|WD=Q966298|lt=Kusunoki Masatsura|1=Kusunoki Masatsura}} along with 24 other [[kami]]. It is one of the [[List of the Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration|Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]]. ==Overview== {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Shijonawate Shrine | image =

Its main festival is held annually on February 12. It was founded in 1890, and enshrines {{ill|Kusunoki Masatsura|en|Kusunoki Masatsura|WD=Q966298|lt=Kusunoki Masatsura|1=Kusunoki Masatsura}} along with 24 other [[kami]]. It is one of the [[List of the Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration|Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]]. ==Overview== {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Shijonawate Shrine | image =

[[Beppyo Shrine]] | established = {{ill|1890|en|1890|WD=Q7832|lt=1890|1=1890}} (Meiji 23) | main hall = | alternate names = | Pilgrimage = [[Kenmu Chuko Fifteen Shrines]]
[[Naniwa Seven Fortunes Pilgrimage]]
[[Sacred Sites of the Kinki Region Pilgrimage]] No. 61 (Osaka No. 20) | festival = Spring Grand festival on April 5, Autumn Grand festival on October 5 | ceremony = | map = {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=205|frame-height=200|marker=religious-shinto|type2=shape|fill2=#f5fffa|stroke-color2=#006400|stroke-width2=1|frame-latitude=34.732|frame-longitude=135.649}} {{location map|Japan Osaka Prefecture|float=center|width=205|label=Shijonawate Shrine|position=left|caption=}} }} '''Shijonawate Shrine''' (四條畷神社, Shijōnawate Jinja) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} located in [[:en:Shijonawate|Shijonawate]], {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|en|Osaka Prefecture|WD=Q122723|lt=Osaka Prefecture|1=Osaka Prefecture}}. The shrine was formerly Ranked as a [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Bekkaku Kanpeisha]] and is currently listed as a [[Beppyo Shrine]] under the jurisdiction of the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]]. It is one of the [[Kenmu Chuko Fifteen Shrines]] and is selected as one of the [[100 Famous Green Sites of Osaka]]. The shrine enshrines [[:en:Masatsura Kusunoki|Masatsura Kusunoki]], who fought as a commander for the [[Southern Court (Japan)|Southern Court]] and died in the [[:en:Battle of Shijonawate|Battle of Shijonawate]]. While his father, [[:en:Masashige Kusunoki|Masashige Kusunoki]], is known as the "Great Kusunoki" (大楠公, Dai Nankō), his eldest son, Masatsura, is known as the "Small Kusunoki" (小楠公, Shō Nankō). Locally, the term "Kusunoki-san" often refers to Masatsura or the shrine itself. In {{ill|1975|en|1975|WD=Q2479|lt=1975|1=1975}} (Showa 50), a town named "Kusunoki" was established, and several other places in the vicinity are named after him. The shrine grounds and surrounding areas are known for their cherry blossoms, attracting many visitors during the spring. == deity == * Main deity - [[:en:Masatsura Kusunoki|Masatsura Kusunoki]] Additionally, 24 other warriors of the Kusunoki clan are enshrined: * [[Masatoki Kusunoki]], [[:en:Masaie Kusunoki|Masaie Kusunoki]], sons of Masaie Kusunoki * [[Yoshihide Wada]], [[Masatomo Wada]], Wada Kirokuzaemon, sons of Wada Kirokuzaemon * [[Iku Hisahide]], Hatakeyama Yosanosuke Toshiaki, Hatakeyama Rokurō, [[Noda Castle (Kawachi Province)|Noda Shiro]], sons of Noda Shiro * Kane no Shō (某), younger brother of Kane no Shō, Seki Jū Ryōen, son of Seki Jū Ryōen, [[Nishi A (name)|Miwa Nishi A]], son of Miwa Nishi A * Kawabe Ishikimaru, Hōda (某), Amaryōgan, Aoya Gyōbu == History == In {{ill|1348|en|1348|WD=Q6085|lt=1348|1=1348}} (Shōhei 3), the [[:en:Battle of Shijonawate|Battle of Shijonawate]] took place here, resulting in the defeat of the Southern Court's forces by the Northern Court's army led by the brothers [[Tadayoshi Ashikaga|Tadayoshi]] and [[:en:Takauji Ashikaga|Takauji]]. Masatsura Kusunoki and his brother [[Masatoki Kusunoki|Masatoki]] committed suicide, along with many others from the Kusunoki clan. Afterward, a tomb called "Kusunotsuka" was built for Masatsura in Nanino Village, and his spirit was quietly enshrined. In the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era|1=Meiji era}}, the government recognized the Southern Court as the legitimate ruling entity, leading to the deification of Masatsura's father, [[:en:Masashige Kusunoki|Masashige Kusunoki]], as the "Great Kusunoki" (大楠公). Masatsura, who followed his father's will and fought for the Southern Court, was also revered as the "Small Kusunoki" (小楠公). In {{ill|1878|en|1878|WD=Q7776|lt=1878|1=1878}} (Meiji 11), his tomb was renamed "Small Kusunoki Imperial Mausoleum" and expanded. Around the same time, officials from Sumiyoshi Hirata Shrine near Mount Iimori petitioned the government to establish a shrine for the Kusunoki clan. The petition was granted, and on December 16, {{ill|1889|en|1889|WD=Q7831|lt=1889|1=1889}} (Meiji 22), the founding of Shijonawate Shrine and its designation as a [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Bekkaku Kanpeisha]] were decreed[{{NDLDC|2945191}} Meiji 22 December 17 Official Gazette (No. 1942)] Ministry of the Interior Notice No. 61. The shrine was established in {{ill|1890|en|1890|WD=Q7832|lt=1890|1=1890}} (Meiji 23) next to Sumiyoshi Hirata Shrine. The area around the shrine flourished, particularly after the Naniwa Railway opened a line from central {{ill|Osaka|en|Osaka|WD=Q35765|lt=Osaka|1=Osaka}} to nearby Shijonawate Station in {{ill|1895|en|1895|WD=Q7844|lt=1895|1=1895}} (Meiji 28). The name "Shijonawate" became associated with the area, and in {{ill|1932|en|1932|WD=Q18743|lt=1932|1=1932}} (Showa 7), the village of Koka was renamed Shijonawate Village, later becoming Shijonawate City. The surrounding region developed into one of the largest urban areas in northern {{ill|Kawachi Province|en|Kawachi Province|WD=Q1047150|lt=Kawachi Province|1=Kawachi Province}}, a status it maintains today. In {{ill|1948|en|1948|WD=Q5165|lt=1948|1=1948}} (Showa 23), the shrine was included in the list of [[Beppyo Shrine]] by the Association of Shinto Shrines. To the west of the shrine lies the Small Kusunoki Imperial Mausoleum (an Osaka Prefecture designated historic site), and to the northwest is the tomb of [[Yoshihide Wada]] (also a designated historic site). == Shrine Grounds == * main hall * Worship Hall * Statue of Masashige and Masatsura Kusunoki, depicting their farewell at Sakurai - Inscribed with "Loyalty and Filial Piety." * Miboyashi Shrine - Enshrines Kusunoki's mother, Kusako Taji no Mikoto. Sub-shrine. * Worship Place * Statue of Masatsura and his mother Kusako - Inscribed with "Loyalty and Filial Piety." * Torii - Gifted from Ise Grand Shrine in {{ill|1990|en|1990|WD=Q2064|lt=1990|1=1990}} (Heisei 2) to commemorate the shrine's 100th anniversary. * Kusunoki Tenjin Shrine - Enshrines {{ill|Sugawara no Michizane|en|Sugawara no Michizane|WD=Q382005|lt=Sugawara no Michizane|1=Sugawara no Michizane}} * Yugen Reikonsha - Enshrines the spirits of ancient and modern scholars and leaders. Originally enshrined at the Adult Education Training Center on the eastern side of Mount Iimori, it was relocated to the current site on April 7, {{ill|2007|en|2007|WD=Q2024|lt=2007|1=2007}} (Heisei 19). * Kusunoki Memorial Tower - A memorial tower for Masashige Kusunoki. It has an unusual design with two round stones representing the water element, discovered in a mountain in Toyonaka City and moved to its current location in {{ill|1991|en|1991|WD=Q2062|lt=1991|1=1991}} (Heisei 3). * Shrine Office * Rest Area - Once an observation deck, it now offers limited views due to tree growth. * Shijonawate Shrine Hall * Off-site ** Small Kusunoki Imperial Mausoleum (Osaka Prefecture designated historic site) - The tomb of Masatsura Kusunoki, featuring a designated camphor tree. ** Tomb of Yoshihide Wada (Osaka Prefecture designated historic site) == Cultural Properties == === Important Cultural Property === * Sword signed "Kanei" - {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|WD=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period|1=Kamakura period}}, deposited in the Osaka History Museum. === Osaka Prefecture Designated Historic Sites === * Tomb of Masatsura Kusunoki * Tomb of Yoshihide Wada === Osaka Prefecture Designated Natural Monument === * Camphor tree at the tomb of Masatsura Kusunoki == festivals == * Spring Grand festival on April 5 * Autumn Grand festival on October 5 == Pilgrimage Sites == ; [[Kenmu Chuko Fifteen Shrines]] ; [[Naniwa Seven Fortunes Pilgrimage]] ; [[Sacred Sites of the Kinki Region Pilgrimage]] : 60 [[Hiraoka Shrine]] * '''61 Shijonawate Shrine''' - 62 [[Minase Jingu|Minase Shrine]] == Transportation == * 15-minute walk from [[:en:Shijonawate Station|Shijonawate Station]] on the {{ill|Katamachi Line|en|Katamachi Line|WD=Q1073379|lt=Katamachi Line|1=Katamachi Line}} (Gakkentoshi Line) of {{ill|West Japan Railway Company|en|West Japan Railway Company|WD=Q502125|lt=West Japan Railway Company|1=West Japan Railway Company}} == References == {{Reflist}} == Related Books == *[https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/1112175/1/1 Yamada Yonekichi, "History of the Fifteen Shrines of Imperial Restoration," compiled by Nirokushinsho, 1935, pp. 82-85. (National Diet Library Digital Collections)] * Anzu Motohiko and Umeda Yoshihiko (editors and supervisors), "Shinto Dictionary," [[Shinto Newspaper Company]], 1968, p. 32. * {{ill|Eiji Shirai|en|Eiji Shirai|WD=Q17216364|lt=Eiji Shirai|1=Eiji Shirai}} and Toki Masanori (editors), "Shrine Dictionary," [[Tokyo-do Publishing]], 1979, pp. 163-164. == Related Articles == * [[Small Kusunoki Imperial Mausoleum]] - Karasu Minami-cho. * Tomb of [[Yoshihide Wada]] - Minamino 4-chome. * [[Sakurai Station Ruins]] * [[:en:Battle of Shijonawate|Battle of Shijonawate]] * [[Nanko Katsura]] - A rakugo storyteller living in Shijonawate City who was influenced by the Kusunoki story when he took his name. {{Commonscat|Shijonawate Jinja}} {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]s in Osaka}} {{Osaka Green 100 Selection}} {{Shinto-stub}} ==See also== *[[List of the Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration|Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]] ==External links== *[http://ikutamajinja.jp Official website] *[http://www.japantimes.co.jp/news/2014/06/24/national/osakas-ikukunitama-shrine-set-revive-ritual-first-time-70-years/ Osaka's Ikukunitama Shrine set to revive ritual for first time in 70 years] {{Shinto-stub}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} {{Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration}} \ {{translated page|ja|四條畷神社|version=103868821|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[image:Shijonawate Jinja Honden 2018.jpg|250px]] [[en:Shijōnawate Shrine]] [[ja:四條畷神社]] [[en:Shijōnawate Shrine]] [[fr:Shijōnawate-jinja]] [[zh:四條畷神社]] [[fr:Shijōnawate-jinja]] [[zh:四條畷神社]] {{wikidata link|Q10925104}} [[Category:1890 establishments in Japan]] [[Category:Bekkaku Kanpeisha]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Buildings in Shijonawate, Osaka]] [[Category:Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]] [[Category:Kusunoki clan]] [[Category:Kusunoki clan|Shrines]] [[Category:Shijonawate Jinja]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:translated pages with valid en interwikis]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:pages]]

Sub-shrine. * Worship Place * Statue of Masatsura and his mother Kusako - Inscribed with "Loyalty and Filial Piety." * Torii - Gifted from Ise Grand Shrine in {{ill|1990|en|1990|WD=Q2064|lt=1990|1=1990}} (Heisei 2) to commemorate the shrine's 100th anniversary. * Kusunoki Tenjin Shrine - Enshrines {{ill|Sugawara no Michizane|en|Sugawara no Michizane|WD=Q382005|lt=Sugawara no Michizane|1=Sugawara no Michizane}} * Yugen Reikonsha - Enshrines the spirits of ancient and modern scholars and leaders. Originally enshrined at the Adult Education Training Center on the eastern side of Mount Iimori, it was relocated to the current site on April 7, {{ill|2007|en|2007|WD=Q2024|lt=2007|1=2007}} (Heisei 19). * Kusunoki Memorial Tower - A memorial tower for Masashige Kusunoki. It has an unusual design with two round stones representing the water element, discovered in a mountain in Toyonaka City and moved to its current location in {{ill|1991|en|1991|WD=Q2062|lt=1991|1=1991}} (Heisei 3). * Shrine Office * Rest Area - Once an observation deck, it now offers limited views due to tree growth. * Shijonawate Shrine Hall * Off-site ** Small Kusunoki Imperial Mausoleum (Osaka Prefecture designated historic site) - The tomb of Masatsura Kusunoki, featuring a designated camphor tree. ** Tomb of Yoshihide Wada (Osaka Prefecture designated historic site) == Cultural Properties == === Important Cultural Property === * Sword signed "Kanei" - {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|WD=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period|1=Kamakura period}}, deposited in the Osaka History Museum. === Osaka Prefecture Designated Historic Sites === * Tomb of Masatsura Kusunoki * Tomb of Yoshihide Wada === Osaka Prefecture Designated Natural Monument === * Camphor tree at the tomb of Masatsura Kusunoki == festivals == * Spring Grand festival on April 5 * Autumn Grand festival on October 5 == Pilgrimage Sites == ; [[Kenmu Chuko Fifteen Shrines]] ; [[Naniwa Seven Fortunes Pilgrimage]] ; [[Sacred Sites of the Kinki Region Pilgrimage]] : 60 [[Hiraoka Shrine]] * '''61 Shijonawate Shrine''' - 62 [[Minase Jingu|Minase Shrine]] == Transportation == * 15-minute walk from [[:en:Shijonawate Station|Shijonawate Station]] on the {{ill|Katamachi Line|en|Katamachi Line|WD=Q1073379|lt=Katamachi Line|1=Katamachi Line}} (Gakkentoshi Line) of {{ill|West Japan Railway Company|en|West Japan Railway Company|WD=Q502125|lt=West Japan Railway Company|1=West Japan Railway Company}} == References == {{Reflist}} == Related Books == *[https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/1112175/1/1 Yamada Yonekichi, "History of the Fifteen Shrines of Imperial Restoration," compiled by Nirokushinsho, 1935, pp. 82-85. (National Diet Library Digital Collections)] * Anzu Motohiko and Umeda Yoshihiko (editors and supervisors), "Shinto Dictionary," [[Shinto Newspaper Company]], 1968, p. 32. * {{ill|Eiji Shirai|en|Eiji Shirai|WD=Q17216364|lt=Eiji Shirai|1=Eiji Shirai}} and Toki Masanori (editors), "Shrine Dictionary," [[Tokyo-do Publishing]], 1979, pp. 163-164. == Related Articles == * [[Small Kusunoki Imperial Mausoleum]] - Karasu Minami-cho. * Tomb of [[Yoshihide Wada]] - Minamino 4-chome. * [[Sakurai Station Ruins]] * [[:en:Battle of Shijonawate|Battle of Shijonawate]] * [[Nanko Katsura]] - A rakugo storyteller living in Shijonawate City who was influenced by the Kusunoki story when he took his name. {{Commonscat|Shijonawate Jinja}} {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]s in Osaka}} {{Osaka Green 100 Selection}} {{Shinto-stub}} ==See also== *[[List of the Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration|Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]] ==External links== *[http://ikutamajinja.jp Official website] *[http://www.japantimes.co.jp/news/2014/06/24/national/osakas-ikukunitama-shrine-set-revive-ritual-first-time-70-years/ Osaka's Ikukunitama Shrine set to revive ritual for first time in 70 years] {{Shinto-stub}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} {{Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration}} \ {{translated page|ja|四條畷神社|version=103868821|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[image:Shijonawate Jinja Honden 2018.jpg|250px]] [[en:Shijōnawate Shrine]] [[ja:四條畷神社]] [[en:Shijōnawate Shrine]] [[fr:Shijōnawate-jinja]] [[zh:四條畷神社]] [[fr:Shijōnawate-jinja]] [[zh:四條畷神社]] {{wikidata link|Q10925104}} [[Category:1890 establishments in Japan]] [[Category:Bekkaku Kanpeisha]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Buildings in Shijonawate, Osaka]] [[Category:Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]] [[Category:Kusunoki clan]] [[Category:Kusunoki clan|Shrines]] [[Category:Shijonawate Jinja]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:translated pages with valid en interwikis]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

[[en:Shijōnawate Shrine]][[fr:Shijōnawate-jinja]][[ja:四條畷神社]][[zh:四條畷神社]] [[en:Shijōnawate Shrine]][[fr:Shijōnawate-jinja]][[ja:四條畷神社]][[zh:四條畷神社]] [[en:Shijōnawate Shrine]][[fr:Shijōnawate-jinja]][[ja:四條畷神社]][[zh:四條畷神社]] {{Short description|Shinto shrine located in Shijōnawate, Osaka Prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = {{nihongo|'''Shijōnawate Shrine'''|四條畷神社}} | image = File:Shijonawate Jinja Honden.jpg | caption = Shijōnawate Jinja [[Honden]] | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{Coord|34.7324|N|135.649|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity ={{ill|Kusunoki Masatsura|WD=Q966298|1=Kusunoki Masatsura}} | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = | location = {{ill|Shijonawate|WD=Q742160|1=Shijōnawate}} | part_of = {{ill|Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration|WD=Q10289770|1=List of the Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration}}; {{ill|Q11274431|WD=Q11274431|1=Naniwa shichikō meguri}}; {{ill|Shinbutsu Reijō Junpai no Michi|WD=Q10444949|1=Sacred Sites of Kamigata}} }} '''Shijōnawate Shrine''' (四條畷神社, ''Shijōnawate jinja'') is a [[Shinto]] shrine located in {{ill|Shijōnawate|en|Shijōnawate|WD=Q742160|lt=Shijōnawate|1=Shijōnawate}}, {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|en|Osaka Prefecture|WD=Q122723|lt=Osaka Prefecture|1=Osaka Prefecture}}, Japan. Its main festival is held annually on February 12. It was founded in 1890, and enshrines {{ill|Kusunoki Masatsura|en|Kusunoki Masatsura|WD=Q966298|lt=Kusunoki Masatsura|1=Kusunoki Masatsura}} along with 24 other [[kami]]. It is one of the [[List of the Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration|Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]]. ==Overview== {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Shijonawate Shrine | image =
Worship Hall | location = Minamino 2-18-1, [[:en:Shijonawate|Shijonawate]], {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|en|Osaka Prefecture|WD=Q122723|lt=Osaka Prefecture|1=Osaka Prefecture}} | coordinates = {{coord|34|43|56.7|N|135|38|57|E|region:JP-27|display=it}} | deity = [[:en:Masatsura Kusunoki|Masatsura Kusunoki]] and 24 others | Shrine Rank = [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Bekkaku Kanpeisha]]
[[Beppyo Shrine]] | established = {{ill|1890|en|1890|WD=Q7832|lt=1890|1=1890}} (Meiji 23) | main hall = | alternate names = | Pilgrimage = [[Kenmu Chuko Fifteen Shrines]]
[[Naniwa Seven Fortunes Pilgrimage]]
[[Sacred Sites of the Kinki Region Pilgrimage]] No. 61 (Osaka No. 20) | festival = Spring Grand festival on April 5, Autumn Grand festival on October 5 | ceremony = | map = {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=205|frame-height=200|marker=religious-shinto|type2=shape|fill2=#f5fffa|stroke-color2=#006400|stroke-width2=1|frame-latitude=34.732|frame-longitude=135.649}} {{location map|Japan Osaka Prefecture|float=center|width=205|label=Shijonawate Shrine|position=left|caption=}} }} '''Shijonawate Shrine''' (四條畷神社, Shijōnawate Jinja) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} located in [[:en:Shijonawate|Shijonawate]], {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|en|Osaka Prefecture|WD=Q122723|lt=Osaka Prefecture|1=Osaka Prefecture}}. The shrine was formerly Ranked as a [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Bekkaku Kanpeisha]] and is currently listed as a [[Beppyo Shrine]] under the jurisdiction of the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]]. It is one of the [[Kenmu Chuko Fifteen Shrines]] and is selected as one of the [[100 Famous Green Sites of Osaka]]. The shrine enshrines [[:en:Masatsura Kusunoki|Masatsura Kusunoki]], who fought as a commander for the [[Southern Court (Japan)|Southern Court]] and died in the [[:en:Battle of Shijonawate|Battle of Shijonawate]]. While his father, [[:en:Masashige Kusunoki|Masashige Kusunoki]], is known as the "Great Kusunoki" (大楠公, Dai Nankō), his eldest son, Masatsura, is known as the "Small Kusunoki" (小楠公, Shō Nankō). Locally, the term "Kusunoki-san" often refers to Masatsura or the shrine itself. In {{ill|1975|en|1975|WD=Q2479|lt=1975|1=1975}} (Showa 50), a town named "Kusunoki" was established, and several other places in the vicinity are named after him. The shrine grounds and surrounding areas are known for their cherry blossoms, attracting many visitors during the spring. == deity == * Main deity - [[:en:Masatsura Kusunoki|Masatsura Kusunoki]] Additionally, 24 other warriors of the Kusunoki clan are enshrined: * [[Masatoki Kusunoki]], [[:en:Masaie Kusunoki|Masaie Kusunoki]], sons of Masaie Kusunoki * [[Yoshihide Wada]], [[Masatomo Wada]], Wada Kirokuzaemon, sons of Wada Kirokuzaemon * [[Iku Hisahide]], Hatakeyama Yosanosuke Toshiaki, Hatakeyama Rokurō, [[Noda Castle (Kawachi Province)|Noda Shiro]], sons of Noda Shiro * Kane no Shō (某), younger brother of Kane no Shō, Seki Jū Ryōen, son of Seki Jū Ryōen, [[Nishi A (name)|Miwa Nishi A]], son of Miwa Nishi A * Kawabe Ishikimaru, Hōda (某), Amaryōgan, Aoya Gyōbu == History == In {{ill|1348|en|1348|WD=Q6085|lt=1348|1=1348}} (Shōhei 3), the [[:en:Battle of Shijonawate|Battle of Shijonawate]] took place here, resulting in the defeat of the Southern Court's forces by the Northern Court's army led by the brothers [[Tadayoshi Ashikaga|Tadayoshi]] and [[:en:Takauji Ashikaga|Takauji]]. Masatsura Kusunoki and his brother [[Masatoki Kusunoki|Masatoki]] committed suicide, along with many others from the Kusunoki clan. Afterward, a tomb called "Kusunotsuka" was built for Masatsura in Nanino Village, and his spirit was quietly enshrined. In the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era|1=Meiji era}}, the government recognized the Southern Court as the legitimate ruling entity, leading to the deification of Masatsura's father, [[:en:Masashige Kusunoki|Masashige Kusunoki]], as the "Great Kusunoki" (大楠公). Masatsura, who followed his father's will and fought for the Southern Court, was also revered as the "Small Kusunoki" (小楠公). In {{ill|1878|en|1878|WD=Q7776|lt=1878|1=1878}} (Meiji 11), his tomb was renamed "Small Kusunoki Imperial Mausoleum" and expanded. Around the same time, officials from Sumiyoshi Hirata Shrine near Mount Iimori petitioned the government to establish a shrine for the Kusunoki clan. The petition was granted, and on December 16, {{ill|1889|en|1889|WD=Q7831|lt=1889|1=1889}} (Meiji 22), the founding of Shijonawate Shrine and its designation as a [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Bekkaku Kanpeisha]] were decreed[{{NDLDC|2945191}} Meiji 22 December 17 Official Gazette (No. 1942)] Ministry of the Interior Notice No. 61. The shrine was established in {{ill|1890|en|1890|WD=Q7832|lt=1890|1=1890}} (Meiji 23) next to Sumiyoshi Hirata Shrine. The area around the shrine flourished, particularly after the Naniwa Railway opened a line from central {{ill|Osaka|en|Osaka|WD=Q35765|lt=Osaka|1=Osaka}} to nearby Shijonawate Station in {{ill|1895|en|1895|WD=Q7844|lt=1895|1=1895}} (Meiji 28). The name "Shijonawate" became associated with the area, and in {{ill|1932|en|1932|WD=Q18743|lt=1932|1=1932}} (Showa 7), the village of Koka was renamed Shijonawate Village, later becoming Shijonawate City. The surrounding region developed into one of the largest urban areas in northern {{ill|Kawachi Province|en|Kawachi Province|WD=Q1047150|lt=Kawachi Province|1=Kawachi Province}}, a status it maintains today. In {{ill|1948|en|1948|WD=Q5165|lt=1948|1=1948}} (Showa 23), the shrine was included in the list of [[Beppyo Shrine]] by the Association of Shinto Shrines. To the west of the shrine lies the Small Kusunoki Imperial Mausoleum (an Osaka Prefecture designated historic site), and to the northwest is the tomb of [[Yoshihide Wada]] (also a designated historic site). == Shrine Grounds == * main hall * Worship Hall * Statue of Masashige and Masatsura Kusunoki, depicting their farewell at Sakurai - Inscribed with "Loyalty and Filial Piety." * Miboyashi Shrine - Enshrines Kusunoki's mother, Kusako Taji no Mikoto. Sub-shrine. * Worship Place * Statue of Masatsura and his mother Kusako - Inscribed with "Loyalty and Filial Piety." * Torii - Gifted from Ise Grand Shrine in {{ill|1990|en|1990|WD=Q2064|lt=1990|1=1990}} (Heisei 2) to commemorate the shrine's 100th anniversary. * Kusunoki Tenjin Shrine - Enshrines {{ill|Sugawara no Michizane|en|Sugawara no Michizane|WD=Q382005|lt=Sugawara no Michizane|1=Sugawara no Michizane}} * Yugen Reikonsha - Enshrines the spirits of ancient and modern scholars and leaders. Originally enshrined at the Adult Education Training Center on the eastern side of Mount Iimori, it was relocated to the current site on April 7, {{ill|2007|en|2007|WD=Q2024|lt=2007|1=2007}} (Heisei 19). * Kusunoki Memorial Tower - A memorial tower for Masashige Kusunoki. It has an unusual design with two round stones representing the water element, discovered in a mountain in Toyonaka City and moved to its current location in {{ill|1991|en|1991|WD=Q2062|lt=1991|1=1991}} (Heisei 3). * Shrine Office * Rest Area - Once an observation deck, it now offers limited views due to tree growth. * Shijonawate Shrine Hall * Off-site ** Small Kusunoki Imperial Mausoleum (Osaka Prefecture designated historic site) - The tomb of Masatsura Kusunoki, featuring a designated camphor tree. ** Tomb of Yoshihide Wada (Osaka Prefecture designated historic site) == Cultural Properties == === Important Cultural Property === * Sword signed "Kanei" - {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|WD=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period|1=Kamakura period}}, deposited in the Osaka History Museum. === Osaka Prefecture Designated Historic Sites === * Tomb of Masatsura Kusunoki * Tomb of Yoshihide Wada === Osaka Prefecture Designated Natural Monument === * Camphor tree at the tomb of Masatsura Kusunoki == festivals == * Spring Grand festival on April 5 * Autumn Grand festival on October 5 == Pilgrimage Sites == ; [[Kenmu Chuko Fifteen Shrines]] ; [[Naniwa Seven Fortunes Pilgrimage]] ; [[Sacred Sites of the Kinki Region Pilgrimage]] : 60 [[Hiraoka Shrine]] * '''61 Shijonawate Shrine''' - 62 [[Minase Jingu|Minase Shrine]] == Transportation == * 15-minute walk from [[:en:Shijonawate Station|Shijonawate Station]] on the {{ill|Katamachi Line|en|Katamachi Line|WD=Q1073379|lt=Katamachi Line|1=Katamachi Line}} (Gakkentoshi Line) of {{ill|West Japan Railway Company|en|West Japan Railway Company|WD=Q502125|lt=West Japan Railway Company|1=West Japan Railway Company}} == References == {{Reflist}} == Related Books == *[https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/1112175/1/1 Yamada Yonekichi, "History of the Fifteen Shrines of Imperial Restoration," compiled by Nirokushinsho, 1935, pp. 82-85. (National Diet Library Digital Collections)] * Anzu Motohiko and Umeda Yoshihiko (editors and supervisors), "Shinto Dictionary," [[Shinto Newspaper Company]], 1968, p. 32. * {{ill|Eiji Shirai|en|Eiji Shirai|WD=Q17216364|lt=Eiji Shirai|1=Eiji Shirai}} and Toki Masanori (editors), "Shrine Dictionary," [[Tokyo-do Publishing]], 1979, pp. 163-164. == Related Articles == * [[Small Kusunoki Imperial Mausoleum]] - Karasu Minami-cho. * Tomb of [[Yoshihide Wada]] - Minamino 4-chome. * [[Sakurai Station Ruins]] * [[:en:Battle of Shijonawate|Battle of Shijonawate]] * [[Nanko Katsura]] - A rakugo storyteller living in Shijonawate City who was influenced by the Kusunoki story when he took his name. {{Commonscat|Shijonawate Jinja}} {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]s in Osaka}} {{Osaka Green 100 Selection}} {{Shinto-stub}} ==See also== *[[List of the Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration|Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]] ==External links== *[http://ikutamajinja.jp Official website] *[http://www.japantimes.co.jp/news/2014/06/24/national/osakas-ikukunitama-shrine-set-revive-ritual-first-time-70-years/ Osaka's Ikukunitama Shrine set to revive ritual for first time in 70 years] {{Shinto-stub}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} {{Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration}} \ {{translated page|ja|四條畷神社|version=103868821|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[image:Shijonawate Jinja Honden 2018.jpg|250px]] [[en:Shijōnawate Shrine]] [[ja:四條畷神社]] [[en:Shijōnawate Shrine]] [[fr:Shijōnawate-jinja]] [[zh:四條畷神社]] [[fr:Shijōnawate-jinja]] [[zh:四條畷神社]] {{wikidata link|Q10925104}} [[Category:1890 establishments in Japan]] [[Category:Bekkaku Kanpeisha]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Shijonawate, Osaka]] [[Category:Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]] [[Category:Kusunoki clan]] [[Category:Kusunoki clan|Shrines]] [[Category:Shijonawate Jinja]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:translated pages with valid en interwikis]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]
